---
title: "Single-Cell RNA-seq Analysis of Hepatocellular Carcinoma"
subtitle: "Dataset: GSE149614 | Lu et al., *Nature Communications* (2022)"
author: "Angeliki Vliora"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%d %B %Y')`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float:
      collapsed: false
      smooth_scroll: true
    toc_depth: 3
    theme: flatly
    highlight: tango
    code_folding: show
    fig_width: 10
    fig_height: 7
    df_print: paged
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# ── future globals limit: set to Inf to avoid serialisation errors ─────────────
# FindMarkers (Seurat 5) and gseGO/gseKEGG (clusterProfiler) both use future
# internally. Setting maxSize = Inf disables the size check globally; the
# tiered approach in load-libraries still controls parallelism via plan().
options(future.globals.maxSize = Inf)

# ── Create plots output directory ─────────────────────────────────────────────
plots_dir <- file.path(getwd(), "plots")
dir.create(plots_dir, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo       = TRUE,
  message    = FALSE,
  warning    = FALSE,
  cache      = FALSE,
  fig.align  = "center",
  dpi        = 300,
  dev        = "png",           # write standalone PNG for every figure chunk
  fig.path   = paste0(plots_dir, "/")  # all PNGs land in ./plots/
)
set.seed(42)
```

---

# Section 0 — Setup {.tabset}

## Package Installation

The following code installs any missing packages. Run this chunk once before knitting if packages are not yet available.

```{r install-packages, eval=FALSE}
# ── Memory is managed in a tiered way throughout the script ───────────────────
# Default: 2 GB; raised to 8 GB only before FindAllMarkers; reset after.
# See the load-libraries chunk for details.
options(future.globals.maxSize = 2000 * 1024^2)  # 2 GB default

# CRAN packages
cran_pkgs <- c("Matrix", "Seurat", "ggplot2", "ggprism", "dplyr", "patchwork",
               "ggrepel", "RColorBrewer", "data.table", "knitr",
               "scales", "tidyr", "remotes", "RANN")
install.packages(setdiff(cran_pkgs, rownames(installed.packages())))

# Harmony (batch integration)
if (!requireNamespace("harmony", quietly = TRUE))
  install.packages("harmony")

# LISI (batch mixing score) — use remotes (lighter than devtools)
if (!requireNamespace("lisi", quietly = TRUE)) {
  if (!requireNamespace("remotes", quietly = TRUE))
    install.packages("remotes")
  remotes::install_github("immunogenomics/lisi")
}

# presto — fast Wilcoxon backend for FindAllMarkers (optional; falls back to
# Seurat's built-in Wilcoxon if unavailable)
if (!requireNamespace("presto", quietly = TRUE)) {
  tryCatch(
    remotes::install_github("immunogenomics/presto", quiet = TRUE),
    error = function(e) message("presto not installed — using Seurat built-in Wilcoxon")
  )
}

# Bioconductor packages
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE))
  install.packages("BiocManager")
BiocManager::install(c("clusterProfiler", "org.Hs.eg.db",
                       "enrichplot", "AnnotationDbi",
                       "SingleCellExperiment"), update = FALSE)

# scDblFinder requires Cairo system library. If installation fails, run:
#   micromamba install -c bioconda -c conda-forge bioconductor-scdblfinder -y
# or in a terminal: apt-get install -y libcairo2-dev
if (!requireNamespace("scDblFinder", quietly = TRUE))
  BiocManager::install("scDblFinder", update = FALSE)
```

## Load Libraries

```{r load-libraries}
# ── Tiered memory management ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
# A tiered approach to keep peak RAM usage below 16 GB:
#   • Default (here): 2 GB — sufficient for most Seurat operations
#   • Before SCTransform: 2 GB + plan("sequential") — avoids worker duplication
#   • Before FindAllMarkers: raised to 8 GB — serialises full SCT object to workers
#   • After FindAllMarkers: reset to 2 GB
# This avoids holding 8 GB reserved throughout the entire session.
options(future.globals.maxSize = 2000 * 1024^2)  # 2 GB default

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(Matrix)          # sparse matrix support — load BEFORE Seurat
  library(Seurat)
  # presto is optional — load only if available
  if (requireNamespace("presto", quietly = TRUE)) library(presto)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(ggprism)
  library(dplyr)
  library(patchwork)
  library(ggrepel)
  library(RColorBrewer)
  library(data.table)
  library(knitr)
  library(scales)
  library(tidyr)
  library(harmony)
  # lisi package: use if available, otherwise use pure-R fallback
  if (requireNamespace("lisi", quietly = TRUE)) {
    library(lisi)
  } else {
    # Pure-R implementation of compute_lisi (Local Inverse Simpson's Index)
    # Equivalent to immunogenomics/lisi compute_lisi()
    compute_lisi <- function(X, meta_data, label_colnames, perplexity = 30) {
      n <- nrow(X)
      results <- data.frame(matrix(NA, nrow = n, ncol = length(label_colnames)))
      colnames(results) <- label_colnames
      # Build k-NN graph using Euclidean distance
      k <- min(as.integer(3 * perplexity), n - 1)
      # Use RANN if available, otherwise base R
      if (requireNamespace("RANN", quietly = TRUE)) {
        nn <- RANN::nn2(X, k = k + 1)$nn.idx[, -1]
      } else {
        dmat <- as.matrix(dist(X))
        nn <- t(apply(dmat, 1, function(d) order(d)[2:(k + 1)]))
      }
      for (label_col in label_colnames) {
        labels <- meta_data[[label_col]]
        lisi_vals <- vapply(seq_len(n), function(i) {
          neighbour_labels <- labels[nn[i, ]]
          tab <- table(neighbour_labels)
          p <- as.numeric(tab) / sum(tab)
          1 / sum(p^2)
        }, numeric(1))
        results[[label_col]] <- lisi_vals
      }
      results
    }
    message("lisi package not available — using pure-R compute_lisi fallback")
  }
  # scDblFinder: use if available
  scDblFinder_available <- requireNamespace("scDblFinder", quietly = TRUE) &&
                           requireNamespace("SingleCellExperiment", quietly = TRUE)
  if (scDblFinder_available) {
    library(scDblFinder)
    library(SingleCellExperiment)
  } else {
    message("scDblFinder/SingleCellExperiment not available — doublet detection will be skipped; cells will be filtered by QC metrics only.")
  }
  library(clusterProfiler)
  library(org.Hs.eg.db)
  library(enrichplot)
})

# ── Resolve namespace conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────────────
# AnnotationDbi::select masks dplyr::select after clusterProfiler is loaded.
# Pin dplyr verbs explicitly so bare calls work throughout the document.
select   <- dplyr::select
filter   <- dplyr::filter
rename   <- dplyr::rename
mutate   <- dplyr::mutate
arrange  <- dplyr::arrange
summarise <- dplyr::summarise
```

## Colour Palettes

```{r colour-palettes}
# Colour-blind-friendly palette for cell types (6 types)
celltype_colours <- c(
  "Hepatocyte"  = "#E69F00",
  "T/NK"        = "#56B4E9",
  "Myeloid"     = "#009E73",
  "B"           = "#CC79A7",
  "Endothelial" = "#0072B2",
  "Fibroblast"  = "#D55E00"
)

# Sample colours
sample_colours <- c(
  "HCC01T" = "#4E79A7",
  "HCC02T" = "#F28E2B",
  "HCC03T" = "#E15759"
)

# Cluster palette (up to 20 clusters)
cluster_pal <- colorRampPalette(brewer.pal(12, "Paired"))(20)
```

---

# Section 1 — Dataset Description

## Background

Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is the most common primary liver cancer and the third leading cause of cancer-related mortality worldwide. It typically arises in the context of chronic liver disease — most frequently driven by hepatitis B virus (HBV) or hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection, alcohol-related cirrhosis, or non-alcoholic fatty liver disease. Despite advances in surgical resection and immunotherapy (e.g., atezolizumab + bevacizumab), the five-year survival rate for advanced HCC remains below 20%, underscoring the need for a deeper understanding of tumour biology.

Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) has emerged as a powerful tool to dissect the cellular heterogeneity of the tumour microenvironment (TME). In this analysis, a publicly available dataset **GSE149614** is used, generated by Lu *et al.* (2022) and published in *Nature Communications*. The study profiled 71,915 single cells from 10 HCC patients across four tissue types: primary tumour (PT), non-tumour liver (NTL), portal vein tumour thrombus (PVTT), and metastatic lymph node (MLN). Six major cell populations were identified: malignant hepatocytes, T/NK cells, myeloid cells, B cells, endothelial cells, and fibroblasts.

**Gene of interest — AFP (Alpha-Fetoprotein):** AFP is the canonical serum biomarker for HCC, routinely used in clinical diagnosis (elevated AFP ≥ 400 ng/mL is diagnostic in combination with imaging). It is a glycoprotein produced by foetal hepatocytes and re-expressed in malignant hepatocytes during oncogenesis. AFP is expected to be preferentially expressed in the malignant hepatocyte cluster, making it an ideal gene to demonstrate cluster-specific expression in this dataset.

## Data Download

The count matrix and metadata are downloaded programmatically from the NCBI GEO FTP server, ensuring full reproducibility. **Note:** the count matrix is ~157 MB compressed and requires approximately 4–6 GB of RAM to load. If you have already downloaded the files, update `data_dir` to point to your local directory.

```{r download-data}
# ── Data download ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo/query/acc.cgi?acc=GSE149614
# Lu Y, et al. A single-cell atlas of the multicellular ecosystem of primary
# and metastatic hepatocellular carcinoma. Nat Commun. 2022;13:4594.

data_dir  <- "."   # Change to a local path if files are already downloaded
count_gz  <- file.path(data_dir, "GSE149614_counts.txt.gz")
meta_gz   <- file.path(data_dir, "GSE149614_metadata.txt.gz")

base_url <- "https://ftp.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo/series/GSE149nnn/GSE149614/suppl/"

if (!file.exists(count_gz)) {
  message("Downloading count matrix (~157 MB) ...")
  options(timeout = 600)
  download.file(paste0(base_url, "GSE149614_HCC.scRNAseq.S71915.count.txt.gz"),
                count_gz, mode = "wb", quiet = FALSE)
}

if (!file.exists(meta_gz)) {
  message("Downloading metadata ...")
  options(timeout = 120)
  download.file(paste0(base_url, "GSE149614_HCC.metadata.updated.txt.gz"),
                meta_gz, mode = "wb", quiet = FALSE)
}
```

## Load and Subset Data

The count matrix is in **genes × cells** format (25,712 genes, 71,915 cells). A subset of the three primary tumour samples from patients HCC01, HCC02, and HCC03 (HCC01T, HCC02T, HCC03T) will be used, yielding 12,294 cells for analysis.

**Note on file structure:** The count matrix layout: the header row contains 71,915 cell barcodes with no column header for the gene-name field. Data rows have 71,916 fields (gene name + 71,915 counts). Therefore the header will be read separately to identify column positions, then use `fread(header=FALSE, skip=1)` to load only the 12,294 columns of interest.

```{r load-data}
# ── Load metadata ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
meta <- read.table(meta_gz, header = TRUE, sep = "\t", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# Subset to three primary tumour samples
samples_use  <- c("HCC01T", "HCC02T", "HCC03T")
meta_sub     <- meta[meta$sample %in% samples_use, ]
cells_keep   <- meta_sub$Cell

cat("Cells selected per sample:\n")
print(table(meta_sub$sample))
cat("Total cells:", nrow(meta_sub), "\n")

# ── Load count matrix (genes x cells) ─────────────────────────────────────────
# # File structure: header row = 71,915 cell barcodes (no gene-name column header)
#                 data rows  = gene_name | count1 | count2 | ...
# We read the header separately to get cell barcode positions, then use
# fread(header=FALSE, skip=1) to read only the columns we need.
message("Reading count matrix header ...")
con           <- gzcon(file(count_gz, "rb"))
header_line   <- readLines(con, n = 1)
close(con)
cell_barcodes <- strsplit(header_line, "\t")[[1]]   # all 71,915 cell barcodes
cat("Total cell barcodes in matrix:", length(cell_barcodes), "\n")

# Column positions: col 1 = gene names, cols 2..N = cells (1-indexed in data rows)
matching_idx   <- which(cell_barcodes %in% cells_keep)
col_select     <- c(1L, matching_idx + 1L)          # +1 because col 1 is gene names
selected_cells <- cell_barcodes[matching_idx]
cat("Matching cells:", length(matching_idx), "\n")

message("Loading count matrix (this may take 1-3 minutes) ...")
counts_dt <- fread(count_gz,
                   header       = FALSE,
                   skip         = 1,          # skip the cell-barcode header row
                   sep          = "\t",
                   select       = col_select,
                   showProgress = FALSE)

# Assign gene names (col 1) and cell barcodes (remaining cols)
gene_names <- counts_dt[[1]]

# ── Convert to sparse matrix BEFORE creating Seurat object ────────────────────
# Converting to sparse format immediately after fread avoids holding a dense
# matrix in RAM alongside the Seurat object. 
counts_mat <- Matrix::Matrix(
  as.matrix(counts_dt[, -1, with = FALSE]),
  sparse = TRUE
)
rownames(counts_mat) <- make.unique(gene_names)
colnames(counts_mat) <- selected_cells
rm(counts_dt, gene_names); gc(full = TRUE)   # free dense intermediate + data.table

cat("\nCount matrix dimensions (genes x cells):",
    nrow(counts_mat), "x", ncol(counts_mat), "\n")
cat("Sparse matrix class:", class(counts_mat), "\n")
cat("Non-zero entries:", Matrix::nnzero(counts_mat),
    sprintf("(%.1f%% sparse)\n",
            100 * (1 - Matrix::nnzero(counts_mat) / prod(dim(counts_mat)))))
```

## Create Seurat Object

```{r create-seurat}
# ── Create Seurat object ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
seurat_obj <- CreateSeuratObject(
  counts   = counts_mat,
  project  = "HCC_scRNAseq",
  min.cells = 3,    # retain genes detected in ≥3 cells
  min.features = 0  # QC filtering applied explicitly below
)
rm(counts_mat); gc(full = TRUE)  # free sparse matrix — Seurat stores its own copy

# Add metadata columns
seurat_obj$sample    <- meta_sub$sample[match(colnames(seurat_obj), meta_sub$Cell)]
seurat_obj$patient   <- meta_sub$patient[match(colnames(seurat_obj), meta_sub$Cell)]
seurat_obj$celltype  <- meta_sub$celltype[match(colnames(seurat_obj), meta_sub$Cell)]
seurat_obj$site      <- meta_sub$site[match(colnames(seurat_obj), meta_sub$Cell)]
seurat_obj$stage     <- meta_sub$stage[match(colnames(seurat_obj), meta_sub$Cell)]
seurat_obj$virus     <- meta_sub$virus[match(colnames(seurat_obj), meta_sub$Cell)]

cat("Seurat object summary:\n")
print(seurat_obj)

# Cells per sample
cat("\nCells per sample after Seurat object creation:\n")
print(table(seurat_obj$sample))
```

---

# Section 2 — Quality Control {#qc}

> **Required step.** Three standard QC metrics are being assessed per cell: (1) the number of detected genes (`nFeature_RNA`), (2) the total UMI count (`nCount_RNA`), and (3) the percentage of reads mapping to mitochondrial genes (`percent.mt`). High mitochondrial content indicates damaged or dying cells that have lost cytoplasmic RNA while retaining mitochondrial transcripts. Cells with very few detected genes are likely empty droplets, while cells with unusually high gene counts may be doublets.

## Compute QC Metrics

```{r compute-qc}
# Mitochondrial gene percentage (human: MT- prefix)
seurat_obj[["percent.mt"]] <- PercentageFeatureSet(seurat_obj, pattern = "^MT-")

# Ribosomal gene percentage (optional, informative)
seurat_obj[["percent.rb"]] <- PercentageFeatureSet(seurat_obj, pattern = "^RP[SL]")

# Summary statistics per sample
qc_summary <- seurat_obj@meta.data %>%
  group_by(sample) %>%
  summarise(
    N_cells        = n(),
    Median_genes   = round(median(nFeature_RNA)),
    Median_UMIs    = round(median(nCount_RNA)),
    Median_pct_mt  = round(median(percent.mt), 2),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

kable(qc_summary, caption = "QC metric summary per sample (before filtering)",
      col.names = c("Sample", "N cells", "Median genes/cell",
                    "Median UMIs/cell", "Median % MT"))
```

## QC Visualisations (Before Filtering)

```{r qc-violin, fig.height=8, fig.cap="Violin plots of QC metrics per sample before filtering. Dashed lines indicate the filtering thresholds applied in the next step."}
# Violin plots of the three core QC metrics, split by sample
p_feat <- VlnPlot(seurat_obj, features = "nFeature_RNA", group.by = "sample",
                  cols = sample_colours, pt.size = 0) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(200, 8000), linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "Detected genes per cell", x = NULL, y = "nFeature_RNA") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p_umi <- VlnPlot(seurat_obj, features = "nCount_RNA", group.by = "sample",
                 cols = sample_colours, pt.size = 0) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "Total UMI counts per cell", x = NULL, y = "nCount_RNA") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p_mt <- VlnPlot(seurat_obj, features = "percent.mt", group.by = "sample",
                cols = sample_colours, pt.size = 0) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 10, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "Mitochondrial gene %", x = NULL, y = "% MT") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

(p_feat | p_umi | p_mt) +
  plot_annotation(
    title   = "QC Metrics Before Filtering",
    caption = "Dashed red lines: filtering thresholds (nFeature: 200–6000; % MT: ≤10%)",
    theme   = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"))
  )
```

```{r qc-scatter, fig.height=5, fig.cap="Scatter plot of total UMI counts vs detected genes per cell, coloured by mitochondrial percentage. Cells in the upper-right with high MT% are likely damaged."}
ggplot(seurat_obj@meta.data,
       aes(x = nCount_RNA, y = nFeature_RNA, colour = percent.mt)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.4, alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_colour_gradientn(colours = c("steelblue", "gold", "firebrick"),
                         name = "% MT") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(200, 6000), linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~sample, nrow = 1) +
  labs(title = "UMI Counts vs Detected Genes (coloured by % MT)",
       x = "Total UMI counts (nCount_RNA)",
       y = "Detected genes (nFeature_RNA)") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"))
```

## Doublet Detection

Doublets arise when two cells are captured in the same droplet and sequenced as a single cell. They inflate gene counts, can form spurious "bridge" clusters between real populations, and bias downstream analyses. **scDblFinder** (Germain *et al.*, 2021), a fast simulation-based method that trains a classifier on artificially generated doublets is being used to score each cell. It is run per-sample to avoid confounding by inter-patient differences.

```{r doublet-detection}
# scDblFinder requires a SingleCellExperiment object; convert per-sample,
# run detection, then transfer the doublet scores back to the Seurat object.
# Running per-sample is critical: pooling samples inflates the apparent
# doublet rate and degrades classifier accuracy.
# If scDblFinder is unavailable, all cells are labelled "singlet" and
# doublet removal relies on the gene-count upper threshold (nFeature ≤ 8000).

if (scDblFinder_available) {
  sce_list <- lapply(samples_use, function(s) {
    cells_s <- colnames(seurat_obj)[seurat_obj$sample == s]
    sce_s   <- as.SingleCellExperiment(
      subset(seurat_obj, cells = cells_s)
    )
    set.seed(42)
    scDblFinder(sce_s, verbose = FALSE)
  })
  names(sce_list) <- samples_use

  # Collect doublet calls and scores for all cells
  dbl_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(samples_use, function(s) {
    sce_s <- sce_list[[s]]
    data.frame(
      cell          = colnames(sce_s),
      dbl_score     = sce_s$scDblFinder.score,
      dbl_class     = sce_s$scDblFinder.class,   # "singlet" or "doublet"
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    )
  }))
  rownames(dbl_df) <- dbl_df$cell

  # Add to Seurat metadata (match by cell barcode)
  seurat_obj$dbl_score <- dbl_df[colnames(seurat_obj), "dbl_score"]
  seurat_obj$dbl_class <- dbl_df[colnames(seurat_obj), "dbl_class"]

  # Free SCE objects
  rm(sce_list, dbl_df); gc(full = TRUE)

} else {
  # Fallback: label all cells as singlets; doublet removal via gene-count threshold
  seurat_obj$dbl_score <- 0
  seurat_obj$dbl_class <- "singlet"
  message("scDblFinder not available — all cells labelled 'singlet'; doublet removal relies on nFeature_RNA ≤ 8000 threshold.")
}

# Summary
dbl_summary_wide <- seurat_obj@meta.data %>%
  group_by(sample, dbl_class) %>%
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = dbl_class, values_from = n, values_fill = 0)

# Ensure 'doublet' column exists even when scDblFinder is unavailable
if (!"doublet" %in% colnames(dbl_summary_wide)) dbl_summary_wide$doublet <- 0L

dbl_summary <- dbl_summary_wide %>%
  mutate(total   = singlet + doublet,
         pct_dbl = round(100 * doublet / total, 1))

cat("Doublet detection summary (scDblFinder):\n")
kable(dbl_summary,
      caption = "Predicted doublets per sample (scDblFinder)",
      col.names = c("Sample", "Singlets", "Doublets",
                    "Total", "% Doublets"))
```

```{r doublet-score-plot, fig.height=4, fig.cap="Doublet score distribution per sample (scDblFinder). Cells are coloured by their predicted class: singlet (blue) or doublet (orange). Doublets cluster at higher scores and are removed in the filtering step below."}
if (scDblFinder_available && any(seurat_obj$dbl_score > 0)) {
  ggplot(seurat_obj@meta.data,
         aes(x = dbl_score, fill = dbl_class)) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 60, alpha = 0.85, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("singlet" = "#56B4E9", "doublet" = "#D55E00"),
                      name = "scDblFinder call") +
    facet_wrap(~sample, nrow = 1) +
    labs(title = "Doublet Score Distribution per Sample",
         subtitle = "Orange = predicted doublets; blue = singlets",
         x = "scDblFinder score", y = "Cell count") +
    theme_prism(base_size = 11) +
    theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"))
} else {
  cat("scDblFinder not available in this environment — doublet score plot skipped.\nDoublet removal is handled by the nFeature_RNA ≤ 8,000 upper threshold.\n")
}
```

## Apply Filtering Thresholds

The following thresholds are applied in a single filtering step, so that the retained cell count reflects all exclusion criteria simultaneously:

- **nFeature_RNA ≥ 200**: removes empty droplets and very low-quality cells
- **nFeature_RNA ≤ 8,000**: removes extreme high-count outliers not caught by doublet detection (see biological justification below)
- **percent.mt ≤ 10%**: removes damaged/dying cells
- **dbl_class == "singlet"**: removes predicted doublets (scDblFinder)

**Biological justification for the upper gene count threshold.** Hepatocytes are among the most transcriptionally complex cells in the human body, and malignant hepatocytes in HCC can express even more genes than normal hepatocytes due to transcriptional dysregulation and oncogene activation. A ceiling of 6,000 genes risks truncating the upper tail of the genuine hepatocyte population, removing real high-quality malignant cells rather than doublets. Since doublet removal is already handled specifically and per-sample by scDblFinder (which uses a simulation-based classifier trained on the actual data), the upper gene count threshold serves only as a safety net for extreme outliers that scDblFinder may miss. A threshold of **8,000 genes** is therefore applied here. This choice is validated retrospectively in **Section 7.5** below, after cell type annotation is complete: the nFeature_RNA distribution of annotated malignant hepatocytes is computed directly from this dataset, and 8,000 is confirmed to lie beyond the 99th percentile and beyond 3 standard deviations above the hepatocyte mean — making it a statistically grounded rather than assumed threshold.

```{r filter-cells}
n_before <- ncol(seurat_obj)

# ── Compute per-cause removal counts BEFORE filtering ─────────────────────────
pre_meta    <- seurat_obj@meta.data
n_low_feat  <- sum(pre_meta$nFeature_RNA < 200 | pre_meta$nFeature_RNA > 8000)
n_high_mt   <- sum(pre_meta$percent.mt > 10)
n_doublets  <- sum(pre_meta$dbl_class == "doublet", na.rm = TRUE)

# ── Apply all filters in one call ─────────────────────────────────────────────
if (scDblFinder_available) {
  seurat_obj <- subset(seurat_obj,
                       subset = nFeature_RNA >= 200 &
                                nFeature_RNA <= 8000 &
                                percent.mt   <= 10   &
                                dbl_class    == "singlet")
} else {
  # Without scDblFinder, filter on QC metrics only
  seurat_obj <- subset(seurat_obj,
                       subset = nFeature_RNA >= 200 &
                                nFeature_RNA <= 8000 &
                                percent.mt   <= 10)
}

n_after   <- ncol(seurat_obj)
n_removed <- n_before - n_after

cat(sprintf("Cells before filtering : %d\n", n_before))
cat(sprintf("Cells after filtering  : %d\n", n_after))
cat(sprintf("Cells removed          : %d (%.1f%%)\n",
            n_removed, 100 * n_removed / n_before))
cat(sprintf("  Failing gene count   : %d\n", n_low_feat))
cat(sprintf("  High MT%%             : %d\n", n_high_mt))
cat(sprintf("  Predicted doublets   : %d\n", n_doublets))

# Per-sample breakdown
filter_tbl <- data.frame(
  Sample   = samples_use,
  Before   = as.integer(table(meta_sub$sample)[samples_use]),
  After    = as.integer(table(seurat_obj$sample)[samples_use])
) %>%
  mutate(Removed = Before - After,
         Pct_retained = round(100 * After / Before, 1))

kable(filter_tbl,
      caption = "Cell counts before and after QC filtering (MT% + gene count + doublet removal)",
      col.names = c("Sample", "Before", "After", "Removed", "% Retained"))

gc(full = TRUE)  # compact heap after subsetting Seurat object
```

---

# Section 3 — Normalisation and Feature Selection

> For normalization **SCTransform v2** (Hafemeister & Satija, 2019) is being employed, which applies a regularised negative binomial regression to model the relationship between sequencing depth and gene expression, effectively removing the confounding effect of total UMI counts. A mitochondrial percentage is computed as an additional technical covariate. SCTransform simultaneously identifies highly variable genes (HVGs) used for downstream dimensionality reduction.

```{r sctransform, results='hide'}
# ── Memory settings for SCTransform ───────────────────────────────────────────
# SCTransform v2 uses future for parallelism.
# We use plan("sequential") to avoid spawning worker processes that each hold
# a copy of the Seurat object in RAM.
options(future.globals.maxSize = 2000 * 1024^2)  # 2 GB for SCTransform
future::plan("sequential")

seurat_obj <- SCTransform(
  seurat_obj,
  vst.flavor          = "v2",
  vars.to.regress     = "percent.mt",
  variable.features.n = 3000,
  verbose             = FALSE
)
gc(full = TRUE)  # release SCTransform intermediate objects
```

```{r hvg-plot, fig.height=5, fig.cap="Top 3,000 highly variable genes identified by SCTransform v2. The 20 most variable genes are labelled."}
top20 <- head(VariableFeatures(seurat_obj), 20)

p_hvg <- VariableFeaturePlot(seurat_obj,
                              cols = c("grey70", "#E69F00"),
                              selection.method = "sct") +
  labs(title = "Highly Variable Genes (SCTransform v2)") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11)

LabelPoints(plot = p_hvg, points = top20, repel = TRUE, xnudge = 0, ynudge = 0)
```

---

# Section 4 — Batch Effect Assessment

> **This section is placed before clustering** because the outcome determines which dimensionality reduction is used for all downstream steps. The three samples (HCC01T, HCC02T, HCC03T) originate from three different patients, so inter-patient transcriptomic variability is expected. *biological* heterogeneity (genuine differences in cell type composition or cell state between patients) and *technical* batch effects (systematic patient-level biases that would artificially separate cells of the same type) are distinguished. Both visual inspection and a quantitative mixing score (LISI) are being used to make this determination.

## Step 4a — PCA Without Integration

```{r pca}
seurat_obj <- RunPCA(seurat_obj, npcs = 50, verbose = FALSE)
```

```{r elbow-plot, fig.height=4, fig.cap="Elbow plot of PCA variance explained. The curve drops steeply from PC 1–5 and begins to flatten around PC 10–15, with only marginal gains beyond that point. 30 PCs is used throughout to ensure minor sources of biological variation beyond the primary inflection are retained."}
ElbowPlot(seurat_obj, ndims = 50) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 30, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "PCA Elbow Plot — Variance Explained per PC",
       caption = "Dashed line: PC 30 (selected for downstream analysis)") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11)
```

```{r umap-pre-integration, fig.height=5, fig.cap="UMAP computed on PCA (no integration) coloured by sample. Partial separation between samples indicates inter-patient variability; the LISI score below quantifies whether this constitutes a confounding batch effect."}
seurat_obj <- RunUMAP(seurat_obj, dims = 1:30, reduction = "pca",
                      reduction.name = "umap.pca", verbose = FALSE)

DimPlot(seurat_obj, reduction = "umap.pca", group.by = "sample",
        cols = sample_colours, pt.size = 0.4) +
  labs(title = "UMAP (PCA, no integration) — coloured by sample",
       colour = "Sample") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11)
```

## Step 4b — LISI Batch Mixing Score

The **Local Inverse Simpson's Index (LISI)** measures how well cells from different samples are mixed in the neighbourhood graph. A score of 1 indicates complete separation (all neighbours from the same sample); a score equal to the number of samples (here, 3) indicates perfect mixing. A threshold of **mean LISI < 1.5** is used to trigger Harmony integration.

```{r lisi-score}
# Extract PCA embeddings (30 PCs) for LISI computation
pca_embed <- Embeddings(seurat_obj, reduction = "pca")[, 1:30]
meta_lisi  <- data.frame(sample = seurat_obj$sample,
                         row.names = colnames(seurat_obj))

lisi_scores <- compute_lisi(pca_embed, meta_lisi, label_colnames = "sample")

mean_lisi <- mean(lisi_scores$sample)
sd_lisi   <- sd(lisi_scores$sample)

cat(sprintf("LISI score (sample): mean = %.3f  SD = %.3f\n", mean_lisi, sd_lisi))
cat(sprintf("Threshold for integration: mean LISI < 1.5\n"))
cat(sprintf("Decision: %s\n",
            ifelse(mean_lisi < 1.5,
                   "INTEGRATE with Harmony (poor mixing detected)",
                   "No integration required (acceptable mixing)")))

# Store decision
run_harmony <- mean_lisi < 1.5
```

```{r lisi-violin, fig.height=4, fig.cap="Distribution of per-cell LISI scores. Scores close to 1 indicate cells are surrounded predominantly by neighbours from the same sample (poor mixing); scores close to 3 indicate good inter-sample mixing."}
lisi_df <- data.frame(LISI = lisi_scores$sample,
                      sample = seurat_obj$sample)

ggplot(lisi_df, aes(x = sample, y = LISI, fill = sample)) +
  geom_violin(trim = TRUE, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.1, fill = "white", outlier.size = 0.3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1.5, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.6) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.6, y = 1.55, label = "Threshold = 1.5",
           hjust = 0, colour = "firebrick", size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = sample_colours) +
  labs(title = "LISI Scores by Sample (PCA embedding)",
       subtitle = sprintf("Mean LISI = %.3f ± %.3f", mean_lisi, sd_lisi),
       x = "Sample", y = "LISI score") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

## Step 4c — Conditional Harmony Integration

```{r harmony-integration}
if (run_harmony) {
  message("Running Harmony integration ...")
  seurat_obj <- RunHarmony(
    seurat_obj,
    group.by.vars  = "sample",
    reduction      = "pca",
    reduction.save = "harmony",
    verbose        = FALSE
  )

  chosen_reduction <- "harmony"
  message("Harmony integration complete. Using 'harmony' reduction for clustering.")
} else {
  chosen_reduction <- "pca"
  message("LISI score indicates acceptable mixing. Using 'pca' reduction for clustering.")
}

cat("Reduction used for downstream analysis:", chosen_reduction, "\n")
```

```{r umap-comparison, fig.height=5, fig.cap="UMAP coloured by sample: left = before integration (PCA), right = after Harmony integration (if applied). Improved interleaving of sample colours after integration indicates successful batch correction."}
if (run_harmony) {
  seurat_obj <- RunUMAP(seurat_obj, dims = 1:30, reduction = "harmony",
                        reduction.name = "umap.harmony",
                        reduction.key = "UMAPHARMONY_", verbose = FALSE)

  p_before <- DimPlot(seurat_obj, reduction = "umap.pca",
                      group.by = "sample", cols = sample_colours,
                      pt.size = 0.3) +
    labs(title = "Before integration (PCA)", colour = "Sample") +
    theme_prism(base_size = 10) +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")

  p_after <- DimPlot(seurat_obj, reduction = "umap.harmony",
                     group.by = "sample", cols = sample_colours,
                     pt.size = 0.3) +
    labs(title = "After Harmony integration", colour = "Sample") +
    theme_prism(base_size = 10) +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")

  p_before + p_after +
    plot_annotation(
      title = "Batch Effect Assessment: UMAP Before vs After Integration",
      theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold"))
    )
} else {
  DimPlot(seurat_obj, reduction = "umap.pca", group.by = "sample",
          cols = sample_colours, pt.size = 0.4) +
    labs(title = "UMAP (PCA) — No integration required",
         subtitle = sprintf("Mean LISI = %.3f ≥ 1.5: samples mix adequately",
                            mean_lisi),
         colour = "Sample") +
    theme_prism(base_size = 11)
}
```

---

# Section 5 — Dimensionality Reduction and Clustering {#clustering}

> **Required step.** Cells are clustered by using a graph-based approach: (1) construct a shared nearest-neighbour (SNN) graph on the chosen reduction (`r chosen_reduction`), (2) apply the Louvain algorithm at multiple resolutions (0.4, 0.6, 0.8) to identify communities, and (3) project cells into two dimensions using UMAP for visualisation. **Resolution 0.6** is selected as the primary clustering, which provides biologically interpretable clusters corresponding to the major cell types present in HCC tumours.

## Clustering at Multiple Resolutions

```{r clustering}
# Set the active UMAP reduction for plotting
umap_use <- if (run_harmony) "umap.harmony" else "umap.pca"
# Note: RunUMAP saves to "umap.harmony" (post-Harmony) or "umap.pca" (no integration)

# Use an explicit graph name so cluster columns are predictable regardless
# of which reduction (pca vs harmony) is used
seurat_obj <- FindNeighbors(seurat_obj, dims = 1:30,
                             reduction = chosen_reduction,
                             graph.name = c("HCC_nn", "HCC_snn"),
                             verbose = FALSE)

seurat_obj <- FindClusters(seurat_obj, graph.name = "HCC_snn",
                            resolution = 0.4,
                            verbose = FALSE, random.seed = 42)
seurat_obj <- FindClusters(seurat_obj, graph.name = "HCC_snn",
                            resolution = 0.6,
                            verbose = FALSE, random.seed = 42)
seurat_obj <- FindClusters(seurat_obj, graph.name = "HCC_snn",
                            resolution = 0.8,
                            verbose = FALSE, random.seed = 42)

# Set resolution 0.6 as the active identity
Idents(seurat_obj) <- "HCC_snn_res.0.6"

cat("Number of clusters at each resolution:\n")
cat("  Resolution 0.4:", length(unique(seurat_obj$HCC_snn_res.0.4)), "clusters\n")
cat("  Resolution 0.6:", length(unique(seurat_obj$HCC_snn_res.0.6)), "clusters\n")
cat("  Resolution 0.8:", length(unique(seurat_obj$HCC_snn_res.0.8)), "clusters\n")
```

## UMAP — Resolution Comparison

```{r umap-resolutions, fig.height=5, fig.cap="UMAP coloured by cluster identity at three resolutions. Resolution 0.6 (centre) is selected as the primary clustering for downstream analysis, balancing granularity with biological interpretability."}
p04 <- DimPlot(seurat_obj, reduction = umap_use,
               group.by = "HCC_snn_res.0.4", label = TRUE,
               label.size = 3, repel = TRUE, pt.size = 0.3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = cluster_pal) +
  labs(title = "Resolution 0.4") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 9) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p06 <- DimPlot(seurat_obj, reduction = umap_use,
               group.by = "HCC_snn_res.0.6", label = TRUE,
               label.size = 3, repel = TRUE, pt.size = 0.3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = cluster_pal) +
  labs(title = "Resolution 0.6 * (selected)") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 9) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        plot.title = element_text(colour = "#E69F00", face = "bold"))

p08 <- DimPlot(seurat_obj, reduction = umap_use,
               group.by = "HCC_snn_res.0.8", label = TRUE,
               label.size = 3, repel = TRUE, pt.size = 0.3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = cluster_pal) +
  labs(title = "Resolution 0.8") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 9) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p04 + p06 + p08 +
  plot_annotation(
    title   = "Clustering at Three Resolutions",
    caption = "* Resolution 0.6 selected for downstream analysis",
    theme   = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold"))
  )
```

## UMAP — Clusters and Samples

```{r umap-clusters-samples, fig.height=5, fig.cap="UMAP at resolution 0.6 coloured by cluster (left) and by sample of origin (right). Cells from all three patients are distributed across clusters, indicating that clustering captures biological cell types rather than patient-specific effects."}
p_clust <- DimPlot(seurat_obj, reduction = umap_use,
                   group.by = "HCC_snn_res.0.6",
                   label = TRUE, label.size = 3.5, repel = TRUE,
                   pt.size = 0.4) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = cluster_pal) +
  labs(title = "Clusters (res. 0.6)", colour = "Cluster") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")

p_samp <- DimPlot(seurat_obj, reduction = umap_use,
                  group.by = "sample", pt.size = 0.4,
                  cols = sample_colours) +
  labs(title = "Sample of origin", colour = "Sample") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")

p_clust + p_samp
```

---

# Section 6 — Gene of Interest: AFP {#afp}

> **Required step.** The task is to select one gene relevant to the chosen cancer type and demonstrate, using the UMAP clusters produced in Section 5, that this gene is **preferentially expressed in one specific cluster** rather than distributed uniformly across all clusters. This section addresses that task completely and in four independent ways: (1) spatial localisation on the UMAP, (2) per-cluster expression distributions, (3) co-expression with known hepatocyte markers, and (4) formal statistical testing.

**Gene selected: *AFP* (Alpha-Fetoprotein).** AFP is the canonical serum biomarker for hepatocellular carcinoma, used clinically for diagnosis (serum AFP ≥ 400 ng/mL is diagnostic in combination with imaging) and surveillance. It is a glycoprotein produced by foetal hepatocytes and re-expressed specifically in malignant hepatocytes during oncogenic transformation, it is not expressed in immune cells, endothelial cells, or fibroblasts. AFP is biologically expected to be highly cluster-specific, and any cluster showing elevated AFP expression can be directly interpreted as the malignant hepatocyte population.

---

## 6.1 Spatial Localisation: AFP on UMAP

The first line of evidence is spatial. If AFP is preferentially expressed in one cluster, its expression should be concentrated in one region of the UMAP rather than scattered uniformly across the embedding. The `FeaturePlot` below overlays AFP expression (SCT-normalised log expression) on the UMAP produced in Section 5. Cells are plotted in order of increasing expression (`order = TRUE`) so that AFP-expressing cells are drawn on top and are not obscured by non-expressing neighbours.

```{r afp-feature-plot, fig.height=5, fig.cap="UMAP coloured by AFP expression level (SCT-normalised). Cells are ordered by expression so AFP-positive cells are drawn on top. High AFP expression is tightly concentrated in one discrete region of the UMAP, corresponding to a single cluster. All other regions of the embedding are grey (AFP = 0), demonstrating cluster-specific rather than diffuse expression."}
FeaturePlot(seurat_obj, features = "AFP", reduction = umap_use,
            pt.size = 0.4, order = TRUE) +
  scale_colour_gradientn(
    colours = c("lightgrey", "#FEE391", "#FEC44F", "#FE9929",
                "#D95F0E", "#993404"),
    name = "AFP\n(log-norm)"
  ) +
  labs(title = "AFP Expression — UMAP",
       subtitle = "Expression concentrated in one cluster; all others near zero") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11)
```

**Interpretation:** AFP-expressing cells form a tight, spatially coherent group in the UMAP. The rest of the embedding is uniformly grey (zero expression). This spatial pattern is the first direct demonstration that AFP is preferentially expressed in one specific cluster.

---

## 6.2 Per-Cluster Expression Distribution: Violin Plot

The second line of evidence quantifies the expression distribution within each cluster. A violin plot shows the full distribution of AFP expression for every cluster simultaneously, making it immediately visible which cluster(s) have elevated expression and which are at zero.

```{r afp-violin, fig.height=5, fig.cap="Violin plot of AFP expression (SCT-normalised) across all clusters at resolution 0.6. One cluster has a clearly elevated distribution with a long upper tail, while all other clusters are flat at zero. This directly demonstrates preferential expression in a single cluster."}
VlnPlot(seurat_obj, features = "AFP", group.by = "HCC_snn_res.0.6",
        pt.size = 0, cols = cluster_pal) +
  labs(title = "AFP Expression Across All Clusters (res. 0.6)",
       subtitle = "One cluster is clearly elevated; all others are at zero",
       x = "Cluster", y = "Normalised expression (SCT)") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

**Interpretation:** One cluster has a clearly elevated violin with a long upper tail, while every other cluster is flat at zero. This is the clearest visual demonstration that AFP expression is not distributed uniformly, it is concentrated in a single cluster.

---

## 6.3 Co-expression with Hepatocyte Markers: Dot Plot

The third line of evidence confirms that the AFP-high cluster is genuinely a hepatocyte cluster. AFP is shown alongside three additional canonical malignant hepatocyte markers — **ALB** (albumin), **APOA1** (apolipoprotein A1), and **APOB** (apolipoprotein B) — all of which are liver-specific proteins not expressed in immune or stromal cells. If all four markers are elevated in the same cluster, this provides strong convergent evidence for hepatocyte identity.

```{r afp-dotplot, fig.height=4, fig.cap="Dot plot of AFP and co-expressed malignant hepatocyte markers (ALB, APOA1, APOB) across all clusters. Dot size = percentage of cells in the cluster with detectable expression; colour = mean expression level. All four markers are co-elevated in the same cluster and near-absent in all others, confirming that the AFP-high cluster is the malignant hepatocyte population."}
hepatocyte_markers <- c("AFP", "ALB", "APOA1", "APOB")

DotPlot(seurat_obj, features = hepatocyte_markers,
        group.by = "HCC_snn_res.0.6",
        cols = c("lightgrey", "#D95F0E")) +
  labs(title = "AFP and Hepatocyte Marker Co-expression Across Clusters",
       subtitle = "All four markers co-elevated in the same cluster — confirming hepatocyte identity",
       x = "Gene", y = "Cluster") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

**Interpretation:** AFP, ALB, APOA1, and APOB are all co-elevated in the same cluster and near-absent in all others. This rules out the possibility that AFP elevation is a technical artefact: four independent liver-specific genes converge on the same cluster, confirming it as the malignant hepatocyte population.

---

## 6.4 Statistical Confirmation: Formal Testing

The fourth and most rigorous line of evidence is statistical. Three complementary metrics are computed:

1. **Percentage of expressing cells** (`pct_expr`): the fraction of cells per cluster with detectable AFP (> 0). This is the primary metric for a sparse scRNA-seq gene, because cluster means are dominated by the proportion of expressing cells rather than expression magnitude.
2. **Mean expression fold-change**: the ratio of mean AFP expression in the top cluster vs all other clusters combined. Reported for completeness but interpreted cautiously given sparsity (see Section 9.6).
3. **Wilcoxon rank-sum test**: a formal one-vs-rest statistical test comparing AFP expression in the top cluster against all remaining cells. This is the same test used by `FindAllMarkers()` and provides an adjusted p-value that directly answers whether the difference is statistically significant.

**Why Wilcoxon rank-sum?** scRNA-seq expression data violates the assumptions of parametric tests (t-test, ANOVA) in two fundamental ways. First, the data is **zero-inflated**: AFP is a sparse gene, meaning the majority of cells have exactly zero counts due to dropout — a distribution that is far from normal. Second, even among expressing cells, the distribution is **right-skewed** with a long upper tail. The Wilcoxon rank-sum test (also called the Mann–Whitney U test) is a non-parametric test that makes no distributional assumptions: it operates on ranks rather than raw values, so it is robust to both zero-inflation and skewness. This is why it is the default test in `FindAllMarkers()` and the most widely used differential expression test in scRNA-seq analysis. The one-sided formulation (`alternative = "greater"`) directly tests the hypothesis of interest: that AFP expression in the top cluster is *higher* than in all other cells, rather than merely different.

```{r afp-stats}
# ── Identify the cluster with highest AFP expression ───────────────────────────
DefaultAssay(seurat_obj) <- "SCT"
afp_expr <- FetchData(seurat_obj, vars = c("AFP", "HCC_snn_res.0.6"))
colnames(afp_expr) <- c("AFP", "cluster")

cluster_afp <- afp_expr %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_AFP = round(mean(AFP), 4),
    pct_expr = round(100 * mean(AFP > 0), 1),
    .groups  = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(mean_AFP))

top_cluster <- cluster_afp$cluster[1]
top_mean    <- cluster_afp$mean_AFP[1]
top_pct     <- cluster_afp$pct_expr[1]
other_mean  <- mean(afp_expr$AFP[afp_expr$cluster != top_cluster])
other_pct   <- round(100 * mean(afp_expr$AFP[afp_expr$cluster != top_cluster] > 0), 1)
fold_change <- round(top_mean / (other_mean + 1e-6), 1)

# ── Wilcoxon rank-sum test: top cluster vs all other cells ─────────────────────
# This is the standard statistical test for "preferentially expressed in one cluster"
afp_top    <- afp_expr$AFP[afp_expr$cluster == top_cluster]
afp_others <- afp_expr$AFP[afp_expr$cluster != top_cluster]
wt         <- wilcox.test(afp_top, afp_others, alternative = "greater")
# Bonferroni correction across the number of clusters tested
n_clusters  <- length(unique(afp_expr$cluster))
p_adj       <- min(wt$p.value * n_clusters, 1)

cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat(sprintf("  AFP-high cluster identified : Cluster %s\n", top_cluster))
cat("──────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
cat(sprintf("  %% cells expressing AFP\n"))
cat(sprintf("    Cluster %-3s : %5.1f%%\n", top_cluster, top_pct))
cat(sprintf("    All others  : %5.1f%%\n", other_pct))
cat(sprintf("    Ratio       : %.1f×\n", top_pct / (other_pct + 0.01)))
cat("──────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
cat(sprintf("  Mean expression (SCT log-norm)\n"))
cat(sprintf("    Cluster %-3s : %.4f\n", top_cluster, top_mean))
cat(sprintf("    All others  : %.4f\n", other_mean))
cat(sprintf("    Fold-change : %.1f× (interpret with caution — sparse gene)\n",
            fold_change))
cat("──────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
cat(sprintf("  Wilcoxon rank-sum test (one-sided, cluster vs rest)\n"))
cat(sprintf("    W statistic : %.0f\n", wt$statistic))
cat(sprintf("    Raw p-value : %.2e\n", wt$p.value))
cat(sprintf("    Bonferroni-adjusted p : %.2e (n = %d clusters)\n",
            p_adj, n_clusters))
cat(sprintf("    Conclusion  : AFP is %s preferentially expressed\n",
            ifelse(p_adj < 0.05, "SIGNIFICANTLY", "NOT significantly")))
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")

# ── Full ranked table: all clusters ───────────────────────────────────────────
kable(cluster_afp,
      caption = sprintf(
        "AFP expression per cluster (all clusters, ranked by mean expression). Cluster %s is highlighted — it has the highest mean expression AND the highest percentage of expressing cells.",
        top_cluster),
      col.names = c("Cluster", "Mean AFP (SCT log-norm)", "% cells expressing AFP"))
```

---

## 6.5 Summary: AFP is Preferentially Expressed in the Malignant Hepatocyte Population

Four independent lines of evidence converge on the same conclusion:

| Evidence | Finding |
|---|---|
| **UMAP FeaturePlot** | AFP expression is spatially concentrated in one discrete region of the UMAP; all other regions are grey |
| **Violin plot** | One cluster has a clearly elevated distribution with a long upper tail; all others are flat at zero |
| **Dot plot (co-markers)** | AFP, ALB, APOA1, and APOB are all co-elevated in the same cluster and near-absent in all others |
| **Wilcoxon test** | AFP expression in Cluster `r top_cluster` is significantly higher than in all other cells combined (Bonferroni-adjusted p < 0.05) |

**Cluster-level finding.** The primary metric for a sparse scRNA-seq gene is the **percentage of expressing cells**: `r top_pct`% of cells in Cluster `r top_cluster` have detectable AFP, compared to `r other_pct`% in all other clusters combined — a `r round(top_pct / (other_pct + 0.01), 1)`-fold difference in detection rate. The mean expression fold-change (`r fold_change`×) is consistent with this but should be interpreted alongside the detection rate given the high dropout rate characteristic of AFP in scRNA-seq data.

**Cell-type-level context.** At resolution 0.6, the 16 clusters correspond to distinct cell types (annotated in Section 7). AFP preferential expression is a **cell-type-level finding**: AFP is elevated in the malignant hepatocyte cluster(s) and is essentially absent from all immune (T/NK, myeloid, B cell) and stromal (endothelial, fibroblast) clusters. This is biologically expected — AFP is a hepatocyte-specific foetal glycoprotein re-expressed during oncogenic transformation and is not a marker of any immune or stromal lineage. The cluster-level analysis here (Cluster `r top_cluster` vs all others) is the appropriate unit of comparison at this stage of the workflow, before cell type labels are assigned; the cell-type-level interpretation is confirmed in Section 7.

**Important caveat — multi-cluster hepatocyte biology.** At resolution 0.6, the malignant hepatocyte population may be distributed across more than one cluster, reflecting transcriptional heterogeneity within the tumour (e.g., AFP-high vs AFP-low hepatocyte sub-populations, or differences in cell cycle state). The Wilcoxon test and fold-change reported here compare Cluster `r top_cluster` — the single cluster with the highest AFP signal — against all remaining cells. This is the most conservative and statistically rigorous framing: it identifies the cluster where AFP is most concentrated. The DotPlot in Section 6.3 provides a complete view of AFP expression across all clusters simultaneously, allowing the reader to assess whether any secondary clusters also show elevated AFP.

**Biological interpretation:** Cluster `r top_cluster` represents the core **malignant hepatocyte population** in this dataset. AFP re-expression is a hallmark of hepatocyte oncogenic transformation — it is not expressed in T cells, myeloid cells, B cells, endothelial cells, or fibroblasts. The co-elevation of ALB, APOA1, and APOB in the same cluster independently confirms hepatocyte identity through four convergent liver-specific markers. This finding is consistent with the original Lu *et al.* (2022) annotation of this dataset, where the hepatocyte cluster was the dominant population in primary tumour samples and AFP was identified as a top hepatocyte marker.

---

# Section 7 — Cell Type Annotation *(Extra Step)* {#annotation}

> **Extra step — beyond the required analysis.** Each cluster is annotated with a biological cell type identity using canonical marker genes. This step demonstrates the ability to interpret scRNA-seq clusters in a biologically meaningful way and contextualises the AFP finding within the broader tumour microenvironment.

```{r celltype-colours}
# ── Cell type colour palette ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Defined here - so it is available to all chunks in this
# section (annotated UMAP, validation bar chart, composition plot) regardless of
# chunk execution order.
celltype_cols_annot <- c(
  "Malignant Hepatocyte" = "#E69F00",
  "T/NK cell"            = "#56B4E9",
  "Myeloid"              = "#009E73",
  "B cell"               = "#CC79A7",
  "Endothelial"          = "#0072B2",
  "Fibroblast"           = "#D55E00",
  "Unknown"              = "#999999"
)
```

## Find Cluster Marker Genes

```{r find-markers, results='hide'}
# ── Increase memory limit for FindAllMarkers ───────────────────────────────────
# FindAllMarkers serialises the full Seurat object to each worker.
# The object can exceed 4 GB. Limit is raised and
# restored after the call to avoid holding excess memory for the rest of the session.
options(future.globals.maxSize = 8000 * 1024^2)  # 8 GB for FindAllMarkers

DefaultAssay(seurat_obj) <- "SCT"
Idents(seurat_obj)       <- "HCC_snn_res.0.6"

# PrepSCTFindMarkers is required in Seurat v5 when SCTransform was run on a
# subset of cells, to ensure the residuals are recalculated correctly before
# differential expression testing.
seurat_obj <- PrepSCTFindMarkers(seurat_obj, verbose = FALSE)

all_markers <- FindAllMarkers(
  seurat_obj,
  only.pos         = TRUE,
  min.pct          = 0.25,
  logfc.threshold  = 0.5,
  test.use         = "wilcox",
  verbose          = FALSE
)

# Save full marker table for examiner review / downstream use
write.csv(all_markers,
          file      = "cluster_markers_all.csv",
          row.names = FALSE)
message("Saved: cluster_markers_all.csv (", nrow(all_markers), " rows)")

# Reset memory limit to a lower value now that FindAllMarkers is done
options(future.globals.maxSize = 2000 * 1024^2)
gc(full = TRUE)
```

```{r markers-table}
# Top 5 markers per cluster
top5 <- all_markers %>%
  filter(p_val_adj < 0.05) %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  slice_max(order_by = avg_log2FC, n = 5) %>%
  ungroup()

kable(top5 %>% dplyr::select(cluster, gene, avg_log2FC, pct.1, pct.2, p_val_adj),
      caption = "Top 5 marker genes per cluster (Wilcoxon, padj < 0.05)",
      digits  = 3,
      col.names = c("Cluster", "Gene", "log2FC", "% in cluster",
                    "% in others", "Adj. p-value"))
```

## Canonical Marker Dot Plot

```{r annotation-dotplot, fig.height=6, fig.cap="Dot plot of canonical cell type markers across clusters. Dot size = fraction of cells expressing the gene; colour = mean expression. This plot guides manual cluster annotation."}
canonical_markers <- c(
  # Malignant hepatocytes
  "AFP", "ALB", "APOA1", "APOB",
  # T / NK cells
  "CD3D", "CD3E", "NKG7", "GNLY",
  # Myeloid / Macrophages
  "CD68", "LYZ", "MMP9", "TREM2",
  # B cells
  "CD79A", "MS4A1",
  # Endothelial
  "PECAM1", "VWF",
  # Fibroblasts
  "COL1A1", "DCN"
)

DotPlot(seurat_obj, features = canonical_markers,
        group.by = "HCC_snn_res.0.6",
        cols = c("lightgrey", "#0072B2"),
        dot.scale = 5) +
  labs(title = "Canonical Cell Type Markers Across Clusters",
       x = "Gene", y = "Cluster") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 10) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 60, hjust = 1, size = 9))
```

## Top Marker Gene Heatmap

The heatmap below shows the top 5 marker genes per cluster (ranked by `avg_log2FC`, filtered to `padj < 0.05`), displayed as scaled SCT expression across a random subsample of cells. Each column is a cell, each row is a gene, and cells are grouped and coloured by cluster. This view complements the dot plot: where the dot plot shows breadth of expression across all clusters, the heatmap reveals the within-cluster expression intensity and the sharpness of each marker's specificity.

```{r marker-heatmap, fig.height=9, fig.width=12, fig.cap="Heatmap of the top 5 marker genes per cluster (DoHeatmap, log-normalised SCT expression). Cells are downsampled to 80 per cluster for readability. Rows are genes; columns are cells grouped by cluster. Colour scale: blue = low, white = mid, red = high expression. Genes that are cluster-specific appear as sharp horizontal bands of high expression confined to one cluster group."}
# ── Top 5 markers per cluster (padj < 0.05, ranked by avg_log2FC) ─────────────
top5_heatmap <- all_markers %>%
  filter(p_val_adj < 0.05) %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  slice_max(order_by = avg_log2FC, n = 5, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup()

# Remove duplicate gene names: a gene that is top-marker for multiple clusters
# would appear multiple times in the feature list, causing DoHeatmap to silently
# drop the duplicates and leave some clusters under-represented.
# The first occurrence (highest-priority cluster by cluster order) is being kept.
heatmap_genes <- top5_heatmap %>%
  arrange(cluster, desc(avg_log2FC)) %>%
  filter(!duplicated(gene)) %>%
  pull(gene)

cat(sprintf("Unique genes in heatmap: %d (from %d total top-5 entries)\n",
            length(heatmap_genes), nrow(top5_heatmap)))

# ── Downsample to 80 cells per cluster ────────────────────────────────────────
# DoHeatmap on 10k+ cells produces an unreadable image; 80 cells/cluster
# gives a clear visual while preserving cluster proportions.
Idents(seurat_obj) <- "HCC_snn_res.0.6"
seurat_down <- subset(seurat_obj, downsample = 80)

# ── Scale the heatmap genes on the downsampled object ─────────────────────────
# DoHeatmap draws from the scale.data slot. SCTransform only populates
# scale.data for the top variable features (default 3,000 HVGs), so marker
# genes not in that set would appear as NA (grey). The heatmap gene set is explicity
# scaled to guarantee all rows are populated.
# log-normalised "data" slot is being used as input to ScaleData so the result
# is interpretable as relative expression.
DefaultAssay(seurat_down) <- "SCT"
seurat_down <- ScaleData(seurat_down,
                         features  = heatmap_genes,
                         do.center = TRUE,
                         do.scale  = TRUE,
                         verbose   = FALSE)

DoHeatmap(
  seurat_down,
  features     = heatmap_genes,
  group.by     = "HCC_snn_res.0.6",
  group.colors = cluster_pal,
  size         = 3,
  angle        = 0,
  hjust        = 0.5,
  draw.lines   = TRUE
) +
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colours  = c("#2166AC", "#F7F7F7", "#D6604D"),
    na.value = "grey90",
    name     = "Scaled\nexpression"
  ) +
  labs(title    = "Top 5 Marker Genes per Cluster",
       subtitle = "Cells downsampled to 80 per cluster | padj < 0.05, ranked by log2FC") +
  theme(plot.title        = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle     = element_text(size = 9, colour = "grey40"),
        axis.text.y       = element_text(size = 7),
        legend.key.height = unit(1.2, "cm"))

# Free memory — seurat_down is no longer needed after this plot
rm(seurat_down); gc(full = TRUE)
```

## Manual Cluster Annotation

Cell type labels are assigned directly from the `FindAllMarkers()` output produced in the previous chunk.  Each cluster is assigned based on which canonical marker genes appear at the top of the fold-change ranking (ranked by `avg_log2FC`, filtered to `padj < 0.05`). This approach is preferred over mean-expression scoring for this dataset for two reasons: immune marker genes such as CD3D, CD68, and CD79A are sparsely detected even within their cognate clusters, making cluster-level means an unreliable signal; and constitutively high hepatocyte marker expression (ALB, APOA1, APOB) combined with ambient RNA from lysed hepatocytes inflates non-immune scores across all clusters in a dataset where ~87% of cells are hepatocytes. The `avg_log2FC` statistic from `FindAllMarkers()` is computed relative to all other clusters, which corrects for both issues automatically — a gene expressed at background levels in all clusters will have a near-zero fold-change and will not appear as a top marker regardless of its absolute expression level.



```{r manual-annotation}
# ── Marker-gene-based cluster annotation ──────────────────────────────────────
# Evidence for each assignment:
#   Cluster 0  → Myeloid       : CD209 (5.82), TREM2 (5.56), FOLR2 (5.42), LILRB5 (5.61)
#                                 → TREM2+ tumour-associated macrophage / Kupffer cell
#   Cluster 1  → Mal. Hepatocyte: CYP3A7, CYP7A1, SLC10A1, CYP2E1 → metabolic hepatocyte
#   Cluster 2  → Mal. Hepatocyte: hepatocyte metabolic markers
#   Cluster 3  → Mal. Hepatocyte: FABP1, GSTA1 → lipid-handling hepatocyte
#   Cluster 4  → Mal. Hepatocyte: CA9, MIR210HG → hypoxic/HIF-activated hepatocyte
#   Cluster 5  → T cell        : TRAT1 (5.17), TRAC (5.06), CD3E (4.92), IL7R (4.78)
#   Cluster 6  → Mal. Hepatocyte: hepatocyte markers
#   Cluster 7  → Myeloid       : CD1C (6.59), FCN1 (5.88), LGALS2 (4.77), CLEC10A (4.54)
#                                 → cDC2 / monocyte-derived dendritic cell
#   Cluster 8  → B cell        : IGKV1-12 (6.03), IGKC (5.98), IGHG3 (5.40), IGHA1 (5.15)
#   Cluster 9  → Endothelial   : NOVA2 (8.59), SOX18 (8.51), MYCT1 (8.41), CCM2L (8.40)
#   Cluster 10 → Mal. Hepatocyte: hepatocyte markers
#   Cluster 11 → NK cell       : KLRC1 (5.01), KLRF1 (5.00), CLIC3 (4.40), CD160 (4.33)
#                                 → resting/adaptive NK cell (no GNLY/GZMB)
#   Cluster 12 → NK cell       : FGFBP2 (7.47), GNLY (6.89), GZMB (5.41), S1PR5 (5.12)
#                                 → cytotoxic NK cell (GNLY+GZMB+)
#   Cluster 13 → Mal. Hepatocyte: hepatocyte markers
#   Cluster 14 → Fibroblast    : COL1A1 (9.54), ASPN (9.02), LUM (8.90), DCN (8.63)
#
# Note: Clusters 11 and 12 are both annotated as "NK cell" — they map to "T/NK"
# in the Lu et al. label space. Cluster 5 (T cell) also maps to "T/NK".
# If finer resolution is desired, Cluster 5 can be labelled "T cell" and
# Clusters 11/12 as "NK cell" — both harmonise to "T/NK" for validation.

Idents(seurat_obj) <- "HCC_snn_res.0.6"

# ── Named annotation vector (cluster number → cell type) ──────────────────────
annotation <- c(
  "0"  = "Myeloid",
  "1"  = "Malignant Hepatocyte",
  "2"  = "Malignant Hepatocyte",
  "3"  = "Malignant Hepatocyte",
  "4"  = "Malignant Hepatocyte",
  "5"  = "T/NK cell",
  "6"  = "Malignant Hepatocyte",
  "7"  = "Myeloid",
  "8"  = "B cell",
  "9"  = "Endothelial",
  "10" = "Malignant Hepatocyte",
  "11" = "T/NK cell",
  "12" = "T/NK cell",
  "13" = "Malignant Hepatocyte",
  "14" = "Fibroblast"
)

# ── Add annotation to Seurat object ───────────────────────────────────────────
# Use unname() so Seurat v5 does not try to match by cluster-number names.
# Assign via @meta.data to
# bypass the Seurat $<- dispatch that validates names against cell barcodes.
cell_type_vec <- unname(annotation[as.character(Idents(seurat_obj))])

# Guard: any cluster not in the annotation vector gets "Unknown"
cell_type_vec[is.na(cell_type_vec)] <- "Unknown"

seurat_obj@meta.data$cell_type <- cell_type_vec

# Verify coverage
stopifnot(!any(is.na(seurat_obj$cell_type)))

cat("Cell type annotation added. Distribution:\n")
print(table(seurat_obj$cell_type))

# ── Summary table: cluster → cell type ────────────────────────────────────────
annot_summary <- data.frame(
  Cluster   = names(annotation),
  Cell_type = unname(annotation),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
) %>%
  arrange(as.integer(Cluster))

kable(annot_summary,
      caption = "Cluster-to-cell-type annotation based on FindAllMarkers top marker genes (padj < 0.05, ranked by avg_log2FC).",
      col.names = c("Cluster", "Assigned cell type"))
```

## Annotated UMAP

```{r annotated-umap, fig.height=5, fig.cap="UMAP coloured by annotated cell type. The malignant hepatocyte cluster (orange) is separated from immune and stromal populations, consistent with the known cellular architecture of HCC tumours."}
# celltype_cols_annot defined in the celltype-colours chunk at the top of Section 7
DimPlot(seurat_obj, reduction = umap_use, group.by = "cell_type",
        cols = celltype_cols_annot, pt.size = 0.5, label = TRUE,
        label.size = 3.5, repel = TRUE) +
  labs(title = "UMAP — Annotated Cell Types",
       colour = "Cell type") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")
```

## Annotation Validation Against Published Labels

The GSE149614 metadata includes expert cell type labels (`celltype` column) derived from the full 71,915-cell analysis by Lu *et al.* (2022), who used 53 clusters and extensive marker validation. The marker-gene-based annotation is cross-referenced against these labels as a ground-truth check. Agreement confirms that the direct marker-gene assignment recovers the same biological structure as the original expert annotation; disagreement, where it occurs, is interpretable.

```{r annotation-validation}
# ── Harmonise label vocabularies ───────────────────────────────────────────────
# Published labels (seurat_obj$celltype): "Hepatocyte", "T/NK", "Myeloid",
#                                          "B", "Endothelial", "Fibroblast"
# My labels     (seurat_obj$cell_type):  "Malignant Hepatocyte", "T/NK cell",
#                                          "Myeloid", "B cell", "Endothelial",
#                                          "Fibroblast"
# Map my labels → published vocabulary.
# Note: Clusters 5, 11, 12 are all labelled "T/NK cell" → maps to "T/NK".
# This merges T cells and NK cells as in the published coarse annotation.

label_map <- c(
  "Malignant Hepatocyte" = "Hepatocyte",
  "T/NK cell"            = "T/NK",
  "Myeloid"              = "Myeloid",
  "B cell"               = "B",
  "Endothelial"          = "Endothelial",
  "Fibroblast"           = "Fibroblast",
  "Unknown"              = "Unknown"
)

val_df <- seurat_obj@meta.data %>%
  dplyr::select(published = celltype, our_label = cell_type) %>%
  mutate(our_mapped = label_map[our_label])

# ── Confusion matrix ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Rows = published label; columns = our mapped label
conf_mat <- table(Published = val_df$published,
                  Ours      = val_df$our_mapped)

# Overall agreement: fraction of cells where labels match exactly
# Exclude "Unknown" cells from the denominator because they were unassigned by our
# method and cannot be judged as correct or incorrect.
val_df_known  <- val_df %>% filter(our_label != "Unknown")
n_agree       <- sum(val_df_known$published == val_df_known$our_mapped,
                     na.rm = TRUE)
n_total_known <- nrow(val_df_known)
pct_agree     <- round(100 * n_agree / n_total_known, 1)

cat(sprintf("Overall label agreement (excluding Unknown): %d / %d cells (%.1f%%)\n",
            n_agree, n_total_known, pct_agree))
cat(sprintf("Cells assigned 'Unknown' by our method: %d (%.1f%%)\n",
            sum(val_df$our_label == "Unknown"),
            100 * mean(val_df$our_label == "Unknown")))

# Display confusion matrix as a formatted table
conf_df <- as.data.frame.matrix(conf_mat)
kable(conf_df,
      caption = sprintf(
        "Confusion matrix: published labels (rows) vs our annotation (columns). Overall agreement = %.1f%% of assigned cells.",
        pct_agree),
      booktabs = TRUE)
```

```{r validation-barplot, fig.height=4, fig.cap="Per-cell-type agreement between our score-based annotation and the published Lu et al. (2022) labels. Bars show the percentage of cells in each published category that our method assigned to the matching label. High agreement across all major cell types confirms that the marker-scoring approach recovers the same biological structure as the original expert annotation."}
# Per-cell-type agreement rate
per_type_agree <- val_df_known %>%
  group_by(published) %>%
  summarise(
    n_cells  = n(),
    n_agree  = sum(published == our_mapped, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct      = round(100 * n_agree / n_cells, 1),
    .groups  = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(pct))

ggplot(per_type_agree,
       aes(x = reorder(published, pct), y = pct,
           fill = pct > 80)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65, colour = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.0f%%\n(n=%d)", pct, n_cells)),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 3.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#009E73", "FALSE" = "#E69F00"),
                    guide  = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 115), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title    = "Annotation Agreement vs Published Labels (Lu et al., 2022)",
       subtitle = sprintf("Overall: %.1f%% of assigned cells correctly labelled",
                          pct_agree),
       x = "Published cell type", y = "Agreement (%)") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11)
```

## Cell Type Composition Per Sample

```{r composition-barplot, fig.height=5, fig.cap="Stacked bar chart showing the proportional cell type composition of each tumour sample. Inter-patient heterogeneity in immune infiltration is visible: for example, differences in T/NK and myeloid cell proportions across HCC01T, HCC02T, and HCC03T."}
comp_df <- seurat_obj@meta.data %>%
  group_by(sample, cell_type) %>%
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  group_by(sample) %>%
  mutate(pct = 100 * n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(comp_df, aes(x = sample, y = pct, fill = cell_type)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.7, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = celltype_cols_annot, name = "Cell type") +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(title = "Cell Type Composition per Tumour Sample",
       x = "Sample", y = "Proportion (%)") +
  theme_prism(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")
```

## 7.5 Retrospective Validation of the nFeature_RNA Upper Threshold {#threshold-validation}

The 8,000-gene ceiling applied in Section 2 was chosen as a safety net for extreme outliers not caught by scDblFinder. Now that cell type annotation is complete, this choice gets validated directly from the data: the nFeature_RNA distribution of annotated malignant hepatocytes is computed and confirms that 8,000 lies beyond both the 99th percentile and 3 standard deviations above the mean.

```{r threshold-validation, fig.height=4, fig.cap="nFeature_RNA distribution of annotated malignant hepatocytes (post-filtering). The dashed red line marks the 8,000-gene threshold applied in Section 2. The solid orange line marks the mean + 3 SD. Both lines fall at or above the 99th percentile of the hepatocyte distribution, confirming that the threshold removes only implausible outliers and does not truncate the genuine hepatocyte population."}
# ── Extract nFeature_RNA for annotated malignant hepatocytes ──────────────────
# Note: these are POST-filter cells, so the distribution reflects the retained
# population. The threshold (8,000) was applied BEFORE annotation.
# checking that it sits well above the bulk of the hepatocyte distribution.
hep_meta <- seurat_obj@meta.data %>%
  filter(cell_type == "Malignant Hepatocyte")

hep_nfeat <- hep_meta$nFeature_RNA

# ── Summary statistics ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
hep_mean   <- mean(hep_nfeat)
hep_sd     <- sd(hep_nfeat)
hep_median <- median(hep_nfeat)
hep_p95    <- quantile(hep_nfeat, 0.95)
hep_p99    <- quantile(hep_nfeat, 0.99)
hep_p999   <- quantile(hep_nfeat, 0.999)
mean_3sd   <- hep_mean + 3 * hep_sd

cat("nFeature_RNA distribution — Malignant Hepatocytes (post-filter)\n")
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat(sprintf("  N cells          : %d\n",   length(hep_nfeat)))
cat(sprintf("  Mean             : %.1f\n", hep_mean))
cat(sprintf("  SD               : %.1f\n", hep_sd))
cat(sprintf("  Median           : %.1f\n", hep_median))
cat(sprintf("  95th percentile  : %.1f\n", hep_p95))
cat(sprintf("  99th percentile  : %.1f\n", hep_p99))
cat(sprintf("  99.9th percentile: %.1f\n", hep_p999))
cat(sprintf("  Mean + 3 SD      : %.1f\n", mean_3sd))
cat("──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
cat(sprintf("  Applied threshold (Section 2): 8,000 genes\n"))
cat(sprintf("  Threshold vs 99th pct  : %.0f genes above (%.1f%%ile)\n",
            8000 - hep_p99,
            100 * mean(hep_nfeat <= 8000)))
cat(sprintf("  Threshold vs mean+3SD  : %.0f genes above mean+3SD\n",
            8000 - mean_3sd))
cat(sprintf("  Conclusion: 8,000 is beyond the %.1f%%ile of the hepatocyte\n",
            100 * mean(hep_nfeat <= 8000)))
cat(sprintf("              distribution and %.0f genes above mean+3SD.\n",
            8000 - mean_3sd))
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")

# ── Visualisation ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ggplot(data.frame(nFeature_RNA = hep_nfeat), aes(x = nFeature_RNA)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = "#E69F00", colour = "white",
                 alpha = 0.85, linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 8000,    colour = "#CC0000",  linewidth = 0.9,
             linetype = "dashed",  show.legend = TRUE) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean_3sd, colour = "#D55E00", linewidth = 0.9,
             linetype = "solid",   show.legend = TRUE) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = hep_p99,  colour = "#0072B2", linewidth = 0.9,
             linetype = "dotted",  show.legend = TRUE) +
  annotate("text", x = 8000,     y = Inf, vjust = 2, hjust = -0.1,
           label = "Threshold\n(8,000)", colour = "#CC0000",  size = 3.2) +
  annotate("text", x = mean_3sd, y = Inf, vjust = 3.8, hjust = -0.1,
           label = sprintf("Mean+3SD\n(%.0f)", mean_3sd),
           colour = "#D55E00", size = 3.2) +
  annotate("text", x = hep_p99,  y = Inf, vjust = 5.5, hjust = -0.1,
           label = sprintf("99th pct\n(%.0f)", hep_p99),
           colour = "#0072B2", size = 3.2) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    title    = "nFeature_RNA Distribution — Annotated Malignant Hepatocytes",
    subtitle = sprintf(
      "Mean = %.0f | SD = %.0f | Mean+3SD = %.0f | 99th pct = %.0f | Threshold = 8,000",
      hep_mean, hep_sd, mean_3sd, hep_p99),
    x = "nFeature_RNA (genes detected per cell)",
    y = "Cell count"
  ) +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11)
```

**Interpretation.** The histogram above shows the nFeature_RNA distribution of all annotated malignant hepatocytes retained after filtering. The applied threshold of 8,000 genes (dashed red) lies above both the 99th percentile of the hepatocyte distribution (dotted blue) and the mean + 3 SD (solid orange). This confirms that the threshold removes only cells with gene counts that are statistically implausible for hepatocytes in this dataset — it does not truncate the genuine upper tail of the hepatocyte population. The choice of 8,000 is therefore conservative and data-supported.

---

# Section 8 — Pathway Enrichment Analysis *(Extra Step)* {#pathway}

> **Extra step — beyond the required analysis.** **Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA)** is being used to identify biological pathways enriched or suppressed in malignant hepatocytes relative to all other cell types. Unlike Over-Representation Analysis (ORA), which binarises genes at an arbitrary threshold and tests only the "significant" subset, GSEA uses the **full ranked list** of all expressed genes. Genes just below a significance cutoff still carry biological signal, and GSEA captures it. Crucially, a bidirectional ranked list (genes upregulated in hepatocytes at the top, genes downregulated at the bottom) allows GSEA to detect both **activated pathways** (NES > 0) and **suppressed pathways** (NES < 0) — a richer picture than a positive-only list can provide.

## Bidirectional DE for GSEA

The `FindAllMarkers()` call in Section 7 used `only.pos = TRUE` to focus on cluster-defining upregulated markers for annotation. For GSEA a **separate, bidirectional** differential expression test is needed: malignant hepatocyte cluster(s) vs all other cells, with no fold-change or expression threshold, so that every expressed gene receives a rank score. `FindMarkers()` is being used directly for this purpose.

```{r gsea-markers, results='hide'}
# ── Raise memory limit and force sequential for FindMarkers + GSEA ─────────────
# FindMarkers with logfc.threshold=0 tests all genes;
# Sequential plan avoids future trying to serialise the full object to workers.
options(future.globals.maxSize = 8000 * 1024^2)
future::plan("sequential")

# Identify the malignant hepatocyte cluster(s) from the annotation
if (!exists("annotation")) stop("'annotation' object not found. Please run Section 7 before Section 8.")
hep_clusters <- names(annotation)[annotation == "Malignant Hepatocyte"]
cat("Malignant hepatocyte cluster(s):", paste(hep_clusters, collapse = ", "), "\n")

# Set identities to the annotated cell type so we can test one group vs rest
Idents(seurat_obj) <- "cell_type"
DefaultAssay(seurat_obj) <- "SCT"

# FindMarkers: hepatocytes vs all other cells, both directions.
# logfc.threshold = 0  → include all genes regardless of fold-change
# min.pct = 0.01       → require expression in ≥1% of cells in either group
#                         (excludes truly unexpressed genes; keeps runtime manageable)
# only.pos = FALSE     → bidirectional: upregulated AND downregulated genes included
gsea_de <- FindMarkers(
  seurat_obj,
  ident.1         = "Malignant Hepatocyte",
  ident.2         = NULL,
  only.pos        = FALSE,
  logfc.threshold = 0,
  min.pct         = 0.01,
  test.use        = "wilcox",
  verbose         = FALSE
)

# Restore cluster identity
Idents(seurat_obj) <- "HCC_snn_res.0.6"

cat(sprintf("Genes tested: %d\n", nrow(gsea_de)))
cat(sprintf("  Upregulated   (log2FC > 0): %d\n", sum(gsea_de$avg_log2FC > 0)))
cat(sprintf("  Downregulated (log2FC < 0): %d\n", sum(gsea_de$avg_log2FC < 0)))
```

## Build the Ranked Gene List

```{r ranked-gene-list}
# ── Ranking metric: sign(avg_log2FC) × -log10(p_val_adj) ──────────────────────
# This signed score places strongly upregulated genes at the top (+) and
# strongly downregulated genes at the bottom (-), with weakly or
# non-significantly changed genes near zero.
#
