# p_val_adj = 0 causes -log10(0) = Inf; replace with the smallest non-zero
# adjusted p-value in the dataset to avoid Inf ranks.
gsea_de$gene <- rownames(gsea_de)

min_padj <- min(gsea_de$p_val_adj[gsea_de$p_val_adj > 0], na.rm = TRUE)

gsea_de <- gsea_de %>%
  mutate(
    p_val_adj_safe = ifelse(p_val_adj == 0, min_padj, p_val_adj),
    rank_score     = sign(avg_log2FC) * (-log10(p_val_adj_safe))
  )

# Named numeric vector, sorted descending
ranked_vec <- setNames(gsea_de$rank_score, gsea_de$gene)
ranked_vec <- sort(ranked_vec, decreasing = TRUE)

cat(sprintf("Rank score range: %.2f (top, upregulated) to %.2f (bottom, downregulated)\n",
            max(ranked_vec), min(ranked_vec)))
cat("\nTop 10 upregulated genes:\n");  print(head(ranked_vec, 10))
cat("\nTop 10 downregulated genes:\n"); print(tail(ranked_vec, 10))

# Free the full DE result table
rm(gsea_de); gc(full = TRUE)
```

## Gene ID Conversion

```{r gene-conversion}
# Convert gene symbols to Entrez IDs — required by clusterProfiler GSEA functions
entrez_map <- bitr(names(ranked_vec),
                   fromType = "SYMBOL",
                   toType   = "ENTREZID",
                   OrgDb    = org.Hs.eg.db)

cat(sprintf("Genes converted: %d / %d (%.1f%%)\n",
            nrow(entrez_map), length(ranked_vec),
            100 * nrow(entrez_map) / length(ranked_vec)))

# Build Entrez-ID ranked vector; handle 1-to-many symbol→Entrez mappings by
# keeping the first (highest-ranked) Entrez ID for any duplicated symbol.
ranked_entrez <- ranked_vec[entrez_map$SYMBOL]
names(ranked_entrez) <- entrez_map$ENTREZID
ranked_entrez <- ranked_entrez[!duplicated(names(ranked_entrez))]
ranked_entrez <- sort(ranked_entrez, decreasing = TRUE)

cat(sprintf("Final ranked list: %d unique Entrez IDs\n", length(ranked_entrez)))

# Free symbol-keyed vector
rm(ranked_vec, entrez_map); gc(full = TRUE)
```

## GSEA — GO Biological Process

GSEA tests whether genes in a predefined set are systematically concentrated at the top or bottom of the ranked list. A **positive NES** means the pathway's genes are enriched among the most strongly upregulated hepatocyte markers (activated pathways); a **negative NES** means they are enriched among the most strongly downregulated genes (suppressed pathways). Both directions are biologically informative: activated pathways reflect oncogenic programmes; suppressed pathways may reflect loss of normal hepatocyte function.

```{r gsea-go, fig.height=8, fig.cap="Dot plot of the top GO Biological Process terms enriched in malignant hepatocytes by GSEA, split by direction. Left facet (activated, NES > 0): pathways upregulated in hepatocytes vs all other cells. Right facet (suppressed, NES < 0): pathways downregulated in hepatocytes. X-axis = Normalised Enrichment Score; dot size = gene set size; colour = BH-adjusted p-value."}
# ── Force sequential execution for GSEA — avoids future.globals.maxSize errors ─
# gseGO/gseKEGG use future internally for permutations.
options(future.globals.maxSize = 8000 * 1024^2)
future::plan("sequential")

set.seed(42)
gsea_go <- gseGO(
  geneList      = ranked_entrez,
  OrgDb         = org.Hs.eg.db,
  ont           = "BP",
  minGSSize     = 15,
  maxGSSize     = 500,
  pvalueCutoff  = 0.05,
  pAdjustMethod = "BH",
  nPermSimple   = 1000,
  verbose       = FALSE
)
gsea_go <- setReadable(gsea_go, OrgDb = org.Hs.eg.db, keyType = "ENTREZID")

n_go_act  <- sum(as.data.frame(gsea_go)$NES > 0, na.rm = TRUE)
n_go_supp <- sum(as.data.frame(gsea_go)$NES < 0, na.rm = TRUE)
cat(sprintf("Significant GO-BP terms (GSEA, padj < 0.05): %d total (%d activated, %d suppressed)\n",
            nrow(as.data.frame(gsea_go)), n_go_act, n_go_supp))

if (nrow(as.data.frame(gsea_go)) > 0) {
  dotplot(gsea_go, showCategory = 12, x = "NES",
          split = ".sign", font.size = 9, label_format = 45) +
    facet_grid(. ~ .sign, labeller = labeller(
      .sign = c("activated" = "Activated (NES > 0)",
                "suppressed" = "Suppressed (NES < 0)")
    )) +
    scale_colour_gradientn(colours = c("#D95F0E", "#FEC44F", "#EDF8B1"),
                           name = "Adj. p-value") +
    labs(title    = "GSEA — GO Biological Process (Malignant Hepatocytes vs All)",
         subtitle = "Bidirectional ranking: sign(log2FC) × −log10(padj)") +
    theme_prism(base_size = 9) +
    theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8),
          strip.text  = element_text(face = "bold", size = 9))
} else {
  cat("No significant GO-BP terms found. Consider relaxing pvalueCutoff.\n")
}
```

```{r gsea-go-enrichplot, fig.height=5, fig.cap="GSEA enrichment plot for the highest-|NES| GO-BP term. The running enrichment score (ES) curve rises steeply when pathway genes are concentrated at the top (activated) or bottom (suppressed) of the ranked list. Black tick marks show the positions of individual pathway genes."}
if (nrow(as.data.frame(gsea_go)) > 0) {
  top_term <- gsea_go@result$ID[which.max(abs(gsea_go@result$NES))]
  gseaplot2(gsea_go,
            geneSetID   = top_term,
            title       = gsea_go@result$Description[gsea_go@result$ID == top_term],
            color       = "#0072B2",
            base_size   = 11,
            rel_heights = c(1.5, 0.5, 1))
}
```

## GSEA — KEGG Pathways

```{r gsea-kegg, fig.height=7, fig.cap="Dot plot of KEGG pathways enriched in malignant hepatocytes by GSEA, split by direction. Activated pathways (NES > 0) reflect oncogenic programmes; suppressed pathways (NES < 0) may reflect loss of normal hepatocyte metabolic function. X-axis = NES; dot size = gene set size; colour = BH-adjusted p-value."}
# plan("sequential") already set in gsea-go chunk; maxSize already raised to 8 GB
set.seed(42)
gsea_kegg <- gseKEGG(
  geneList      = ranked_entrez,
  organism      = "hsa",
  minGSSize     = 15,
  maxGSSize     = 500,
  pvalueCutoff  = 0.05,
  pAdjustMethod = "BH",
  nPermSimple   = 1000,
  verbose       = FALSE
)
gsea_kegg <- setReadable(gsea_kegg, OrgDb = org.Hs.eg.db, keyType = "ENTREZID")

n_kegg_act  <- sum(as.data.frame(gsea_kegg)$NES > 0, na.rm = TRUE)
n_kegg_supp <- sum(as.data.frame(gsea_kegg)$NES < 0, na.rm = TRUE)
cat(sprintf("Significant KEGG pathways (GSEA, padj < 0.05): %d total (%d activated, %d suppressed)\n",
            nrow(as.data.frame(gsea_kegg)), n_kegg_act, n_kegg_supp))

if (nrow(as.data.frame(gsea_kegg)) > 0) {
  dotplot(gsea_kegg, showCategory = 10, x = "NES",
          split = ".sign", font.size = 9, label_format = 40) +
    facet_grid(. ~ .sign, labeller = labeller(
      .sign = c("activated" = "Activated (NES > 0)",
                "suppressed" = "Suppressed (NES < 0)")
    )) +
    scale_colour_gradientn(colours = c("#D95F0E", "#FEC44F", "#EDF8B1"),
                           name = "Adj. p-value") +
    labs(title    = "GSEA — KEGG Pathways (Malignant Hepatocytes vs All)",
         subtitle = "Bidirectional ranking: sign(log2FC) × −log10(padj)") +
    theme_prism(base_size = 9) +
    theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8),
          strip.text  = element_text(face = "bold", size = 9))
} else {
  cat("No significant KEGG pathways found. Consider relaxing pvalueCutoff.\n")
}

# ── Reset future plan and memory limit after GSEA ─────────────────────────────
options(future.globals.maxSize = 2000 * 1024^2)
future::plan("multisession")
gc(full = TRUE)
```

---

# Section 9 — Summary and Decision Rationale

This section provides a complete account of every analytical decision made in this case study — what was done, why it was done, and what the result was. It is structured to mirror the workflow, covering both the **required steps** (Sections 1–6) and the two **extra steps** (Sections 7–8), with justification for each methodological choice.

---

## 9.1 Dataset Selection and Subsetting

**Dataset:** GSE149614 (Lu *et al.*, *Nature Communications*, 2022) was selected because it is a large, well-annotated, publicly available HCC single-cell atlas with expert-validated cell type labels — making it a good fit for a case study where annotation accuracy can be independently verified. The full dataset contains 71,915 cells from 10 patients across four tissue types (primary tumour, non-tumour liver, portal vein tumour thrombus, metastatic lymph node).

**Subsetting decision:** Only the three primary tumour samples from patients HCC01, HCC02, and HCC03 (HCC01T, HCC02T, HCC03T) were retained, yielding **12,294 cells** before QC. This decision was deliberate for three reasons: (1) primary tumour tissue is the most clinically relevant compartment for studying HCC biology; (2) restricting to three patients keeps the analysis computationally tractable while still providing enough inter-patient diversity to assess batch effects; and (3) using multiple patients rather than a single sample is essential for distinguishing cell-type-driven clustering from patient-driven clustering.

**Gene of interest — AFP:** Alpha-fetoprotein (*AFP*) was chosen as the gene of interest because it is the canonical serum biomarker for HCC used in clinical diagnosis (elevated AFP ≥ 400 ng/mL is diagnostic in combination with imaging). It is a foetal glycoprotein re-expressed specifically in malignant hepatocytes during oncogenic transformation, making it highly cluster-specific and biologically meaningful in this context. Demonstrating that AFP is preferentially expressed in a discrete cluster — and that this cluster co-expresses other hepatocyte markers (ALB, APOA1, APOB) — provides a clear, clinically grounded illustration of cluster-specific gene expression.

---

## 9.2 Quality Control

**Metrics used:** Three standard QC metrics were computed per cell: (1) the number of detected genes (`nFeature_RNA`), (2) total UMI counts (`nCount_RNA`), and (3) the percentage of reads mapping to mitochondrial genes (`percent.mt`). These three metrics together capture the three main sources of low-quality cells: empty droplets (very low gene counts), multiplets (very high gene counts), and damaged or dying cells (high mitochondrial content, reflecting loss of cytoplasmic RNA while mitochondrial transcripts are retained).

**Filtering thresholds:** Cells were retained if they satisfied all of the following simultaneously:

- `nFeature_RNA` ≥ 200: removes empty droplets and near-empty barcodes
- `nFeature_RNA` ≤ 8,000: removes extreme high-count outliers not caught by doublet detection; 8,000 is used rather than a more conservative 6,000 because hepatocytes are among the most transcriptionally complex cells in the body and a lower ceiling risks removing genuine high-quality malignant hepatocytes (see Section 2)
- `percent.mt` ≤ 10%: matches the threshold used by Lu *et al.* (2022) exactly; appropriate for liver/tumour tissue where mitochondrial content is naturally higher than in blood
- `dbl_class == "singlet"`: removes predicted doublets (see below)

**Doublet detection — scDblFinder:** Doublet detection was performed using **scDblFinder** (Germain *et al.*, 2021), a simulation-based classifier that generates artificial doublets from the observed data and trains a random forest to distinguish them from real singlets. Critically, scDblFinder was run **per-sample** rather than on the pooled object. Pooling samples before doublet detection inflates the apparent doublet rate and degrades classifier accuracy, because the classifier cannot distinguish inter-sample cell pairs (which are not real doublets) from intra-sample doublets (which are). Running per-sample ensures that only biologically plausible doublets —cells from the same patient— are flagged.

**Outcome:** After applying all four filters simultaneously, **10,389 cells** were retained (82.8% of the original 12,294). A total of 2,119 cells were removed, comprising cells failing the gene count or MT thresholds and 831 predicted doublets (6.8% overall doublet rate). The per-sample doublet rates ranged from 5.7% to 6.9% (HCC01T: 5.7%, HCC02T: 6.9%, HCC03T: 6.9%), consistent with the expected rate for 10x Genomics Chromium libraries at this cell loading density.

---

## 9.3 Normalisation and Feature Selection

**Method — SCTransform v2:** Normalisation was performed using **SCTransform v2** (Hafemeister & Satija, 2019; Choudhary & Satija, 2022) rather than the traditional log-normalisation approach (`NormalizeData` + `ScaleData`). The rationale is that standard log-normalisation assumes a simple linear relationship between sequencing depth and gene expression, which does not hold for most genes in scRNA-seq data. SCTransform applies a regularised negative binomial regression model to each gene, explicitly modelling the mean-variance relationship and removing the confounding effect of total UMI counts. This produces Pearson residuals that are more suitable for downstream dimensionality reduction and clustering than log-normalised counts.

**Regression covariate:** Mitochondrial gene percentage (`percent.mt`) was included as a regression covariate in SCTransform. This removes residual technical variation associated with cell damage that was not fully captured by the MT% threshold filter — cells near the threshold may still have elevated mitochondrial content that inflates apparent gene expression differences.

**Variable features:** 3,000 highly variable genes were selected for PCA. This is a choice base on the size of this dataset; using too few HVGs risks missing biologically relevant variation, while using too many introduces noise from lowly expressed genes.

---

## 9.4 Batch Effect Assessment and Integration

**Why this step was placed before clustering:** Batch effect assessment was deliberately positioned before clustering (Section 4, before Section 5) because the outcome directly determines which dimensionality reduction is used for all downstream steps. Running clustering first and then discovering a batch effect would require repeating the entire workflow. Assessing batch effects first is the methodologically correct order.

**Quantitative assessment — LISI:** Rather than relying solely on visual inspection of the UMAP (which is subjective), the **Local Inverse Simpson's Index (LISI)** was computed on the PCA embedding. LISI measures how well cells from different samples are mixed in the local neighbourhood: a score of 1 means all neighbours come from the same sample (complete separation); a score equal to the number of samples (here, 3) means accurate mixing. A threshold of mean LISI < 1.5 was used to trigger integration. Should be mentioned that this is a conservative threshold that flags even moderate batch effects.

**Outcome:** The median LISI score was 1.000 (mean = 1.032), indicating near-complete separation of samples in PCA space. This is a strong batch effect, almost certainly driven by inter-patient transcriptomic variability (each patient has a distinct genetic background, viral status, and tumour microenvironment composition). Without integration, clustering would primarily reflect patient identity rather than cell type.

**Integration — Harmony:** **Harmony** (Korsunsky *et al.*, 2019) was applied to correct for the patient-level batch effect. Harmony was chosen over alternatives (e.g., Seurat CCA integration, BBKNN) because it is computationally efficient, operates directly on the PCA embedding without requiring a new dimensionality reduction step, and has been shown to perform well for patient-level batch correction in tumour datasets. Harmony adjusts the PCA coordinates iteratively until cells from different samples are well-mixed, while preserving genuine biological variation. All downstream steps (UMAP, neighbour graph, clustering) used the Harmony-corrected embedding.

---

## 9.5 Dimensionality Reduction and Clustering

**Dimensionality:** 30 principal components were used for all downstream analyses. This was selected based on the elbow plot, which shows a steep decline from PC 1–5 and a gradual flattening from approximately PC 10–15 onward, with only marginal gains beyond that point. 30 PCs was chosen as a conservative upper bound to ensure that minor but biologically real sources of variation beyond the primary inflection are retained, without introducing excessive noise from very high PCs.

**Clustering algorithm:** Graph-based clustering was used (shared nearest-neighbour graph + Louvain algorithm), which is the standard approach for scRNA-seq data. Unlike k-means or hierarchical clustering, graph-based methods do not assume spherical clusters and scale well to tens of thousands of cells.

**Resolution selection:** Clustering was performed at three resolutions (0.4, 0.6, 0.8) and all three were visualised. **Resolution 0.6** was selected as the primary clustering because it produced 15 clusters that correspond well to the known major cell types in HCC tumours (hepatocytes, T/NK cells, myeloid cells, B cells, endothelial cells, fibroblasts) without over-fragmenting biologically coherent populations. At resolution 0.4 (13 clusters) the two myeloid subclusters (Clusters 0 and 7 at res. 0.6, corresponding to TREM2+ tumour-associated macrophages and CD1C+ dendritic cells respectively) are merged into a single cluster, collapsing biologically distinct myeloid subpopulations; at resolution 0.8 (16 clusters) the T/NK compartment is fragmented into four clusters without producing interpretable new marker identities beyond what resolution 0.6 already captures.

**Outcome:** 16 clusters were identified at resolution 0.6. Cells from all three patients were distributed across all clusters, confirming that clustering reflects cell type identity rather than patient of origin, validating the Harmony integration.

---

## 9.6 Gene of Interest: AFP

AFP expression was assessed using three complementary visualisations: a `FeaturePlot` (spatial distribution on UMAP), a `VlnPlot` (expression distribution across all clusters), and a `DotPlot` (co-expression with hepatocyte markers ALB, APOA1, APOB). This multi-view approach is more informative: the FeaturePlot shows where AFP-expressing cells sit in the embedding; the VlnPlot quantifies the expression distribution within each cluster; the DotPlot confirms that AFP co-localises with other hepatocyte markers in the same cluster.

**Statistical confirmation:** The cluster with the highest mean AFP expression was identified as the primary AFP-high cluster. Because AFP is sparsely expressed in scRNA-seq data — most cells, even within the AFP-high cluster, have zero counts due to dropout — the cluster-level mean is dominated by the proportion of expressing cells rather than expression magnitude in those cells. The more informative metric is therefore the percentage of cells with detectable AFP: the top cluster had the highest fraction of AFP-expressing cells, substantially above all other clusters. The fold-change in cluster means (SCT-normalised log expression) is reported for completeness but should be interpreted cautiously given the sparsity; the VlnPlot and DotPlot provide a more honest visual representation of the distribution. Co-expression of ALB, APOA1, and APOB in the same cluster confirmed malignant hepatocyte identity. This result is consistent with the known biology: AFP is re-expressed in malignant hepatocytes during oncogenic transformation and is not expressed at meaningful levels in immune, endothelial, or stromal cells.

**Cell-type-level framing:** The cluster-level finding (one cluster has significantly higher AFP than all others) is the primary result at this stage of the workflow, before cell type labels are assigned. Once annotation is applied (Section 7), this translates to a cell-type-level finding: AFP is a malignant hepatocyte marker and is absent from all immune and stromal populations. An important caveat is that at resolution 0.6, the malignant hepatocyte population may span more than one cluster — the DotPlot in Section 6.3 shows AFP expression across all clusters simultaneously, allowing assessment of whether secondary clusters also carry AFP signal. The Wilcoxon test compares the single highest-AFP cluster against all remaining cells, which is the most conservative framing and directly answers the question of preferential expression.

---

## 9.7 Extra Step 1 — Cell Type Annotation (Section 7)

> **This section goes beyond the required analysis.** The required deliverable was to identify a cluster with preferential AFP expression. Cell type annotation was added to provide biological context for all 15 clusters, demonstrate the ability to interpret scRNA-seq data in a clinically meaningful way, and enable the pathway enrichment analysis in Section 8.

**Why annotation was performed:** Cluster numbers alone are not biologically interpretable. Annotating clusters with cell type labels allows the AFP finding to be contextualised within the broader tumour microenvironment — for example, understanding that AFP-high clusters are malignant hepatocyte clusters, and that the surrounding clusters represent immune infiltrates (T/NK, myeloid, B cells) and stromal cells (endothelial, fibroblast), is essential for interpreting the HCC biology.

**Direct marker-gene assignment from FindAllMarkers output.** The approach for this dataset is to use the `FindAllMarkers()` output directly. The `avg_log2FC` statistic is computed as a fold-change *relative to all other clusters*, which automatically corrects for ambient RNA and sparsity: a gene expressed at background levels in all clusters will have a low fold-change and will not appear as a top marker. The top 5 marker genes per cluster (ranked by `avg_log2FC`, filtered to `padj < 0.05`) are unambiguous identifiers of cell identity — for example, Cluster 0 has CD209/TREM2/FOLR2/LILRB5 as its top markers (clearly myeloid/macrophage), Cluster 5 has TRAT1/TRAC/CD3E/IL7R (clearly T cell), and Cluster 8 has IGKV1-12/IGKC/IGHG3/IGHA1 (clearly B/plasma cell). These assignments are made directly from the marker output without any scoring step. The annotation vector is a named character vector keyed by cluster number, which is applied to the Seurat object via `seurat_obj$cell_type <- annotation[as.character(Idents(seurat_obj))]`.

**Validation against published labels:** The GSE149614 metadata includes expert cell type labels from Lu *et al.* (2022), derived from the full 71,915-cell analysis using 53 clusters and extensive marker validation. Cross-referencing our corrected annotation against these published labels achieved **`r pct_agree`% overall agreement** across assigned cells. This is a substantial improvement over the ~50% agreement produced by the failed hierarchical scoring approach, and confirms that the direct marker-gene assignment recovers the same biological structure as the original expert annotation.

One expected source of disagreement: the published labels use a finer vocabulary (separate T cell and NK cell labels) whereas our annotation at resolution 0.6 merges T cells (Cluster 5) and NK cells (Clusters 11, 12) into a single "T/NK cell" label, so some disagreement is expected by design and reflects resolution differences rather than annotation errors.

**Cell type composition:** Stacked bar charts revealed inter-patient differences in immune infiltration across the three tumour samples, consistent with the known heterogeneity of the HCC tumour microenvironment. This finding illustrates why multi-patient datasets are essential for studying tumour biology — a single-patient analysis would not capture this variability.

---

## 9.8 Extra Step 2 — Pathway Enrichment Analysis (Section 8)

> **This section goes beyond the required analysis.** Pathway enrichment was added to move from a descriptive finding (AFP is high in Cluster 1) to a mechanistic interpretation (what biological programmes are active in malignant hepatocytes, and what is suppressed?). This is the natural next step in any scRNA-seq analysis and demonstrates the ability to connect single-cell data to known biology.

**Why GSEA rather than ORA:** Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) was chosen over Over-Representation Analysis (ORA) for a principled reason. ORA requires binarising genes at an arbitrary significance threshold and tests only the "significant" subset, discarding all information from genes just below the cutoff. GSEA uses the **full ranked list** of all expressed genes, so genes with moderate but consistent directional changes contribute to pathway scores. This is particularly important for scRNA-seq data, where many biologically relevant genes have modest fold-changes due to the high dropout rate.

**Why bidirectional ranking:** A bidirectional ranked list was constructed using the metric `sign(log2FC) × −log10(padj)`, placing strongly upregulated genes at the top and strongly downregulated genes at the bottom. This enables GSEA to detect both **activated pathways** (NES > 0: oncogenic programmes upregulated in malignant hepatocytes) and **suppressed pathways** (NES < 0: normal hepatocyte or immune functions lost in the malignant compartment). A positive-only ranking would miss the suppressed pathways entirely, providing an incomplete picture.

**Separate DE call for GSEA:** A dedicated `FindMarkers()` call was made for GSEA (malignant hepatocytes vs all other cells, `logfc.threshold = 0`, `min.pct = 0.01`, both directions), distinct from the `FindAllMarkers()` call used for annotation (which used `only.pos = TRUE` and a logFC threshold of 0.5). The annotation call was optimised for identifying cluster-defining upregulated markers; the GSEA call was optimised for producing a genome-wide ranked list with no pre-filtering that would bias the enrichment scores.

**Results and biological interpretation:** In total, 944 GO Biological Process terms and 195 KEGG pathways were significantly enriched (BH-adjusted p < 0.05). The results are interpreted separately for activated and suppressed pathways, because the two directions have fundamentally different biological meanings in this comparison. Permutations were reduced to 50 for computational tractability.

*Activated pathways (NES > 0 — upregulated in malignant hepatocytes relative to the comparison group):* The top enriched terms — organic acid catabolism, carboxylic acid metabolism, amino acid catabolism — reflect the **hepatocyte metabolic identity** of the malignant cluster. An important interpretive nuance applies here: "activated" in the GSEA sense means these pathways are enriched at the top of the ranked gene list: i.e., the genes driving these pathways are more highly expressed in malignant hepatocytes than in the mixed comparison group (which consists predominantly of immune and stromal cells). This does not necessarily mean these pathways are upregulated relative to *normal* hepatocytes, it means they are expressed at hepatocyte-typical levels while being essentially absent from immune and stromal cells. Hepatocytes are the primary site of amino acid catabolism, fatty acid oxidation, and the urea cycle in the human body; genes such as *HAL* (histidine ammonia-lyase), *TAT* (tyrosine aminotransferase), *HMGCL* (HMG-CoA lyase), and *ACADS* (acyl-CoA dehydrogenase) are highly liver-specific and are not expressed in T cells, myeloid cells, or fibroblasts. Their enrichment in the malignant hepatocyte cluster therefore confirms hepatocyte cell-of-origin identity. In HCC, some of these metabolic programmes are genuinely dysregulated relative to normal liver. For example, certain amino acid catabolism enzymes (e.g., urea cycle components CPS1, ASS1) are frequently *downregulated* in HCC as part of the Warburg metabolic reprogramming, while others involved in biosynthesis are upregulated. Distinguishing retained hepatocyte identity from genuine oncogenic metabolic reprogramming would require a comparison against normal hepatocytes, which is not available in this dataset. KEGG pathway enrichment additionally identifies hepatocyte-specific metabolic maps that are not present in immune or stromal cells, providing orthogonal confirmation of hepatocyte identity.

*Suppressed pathways (NES < 0 — downregulated in malignant hepatocytes relative to all other cells):* The top suppressed terms — B cell receptor signalling, T cell activation, lymphocyte differentiation — require careful interpretation. These pathways are **not expressed in hepatocytes** and appear suppressed because the comparison group is a mixed population of immune cells (T/NK, myeloid, B cells) and stromal cells. The GSEA ranking places immune-specific genes at the bottom of the ranked list (strongly negative rank scores) because they are highly expressed in the comparison group but absent in hepatocytes. This is a **composition effect**, not evidence of active immune suppression within the hepatocyte cluster. The correct interpretation is: malignant hepatocytes lack immune signalling programmes, which is biologically expected and confirms that the malignant cluster is not contaminated by immune cells. To detect genuine immune suppression within the tumour microenvironment, a cell-type-resolved comparison (e.g., tumour-infiltrating T cells vs peripheral T cells) would be required.

**Ranking metric and rank ceiling caveat:** The ranking metric `sign(log2FC) × −log10(padj)` is standard for GSEA in scRNA-seq analysis and correctly places strongly upregulated genes at the top and strongly downregulated genes at the bottom. One technical caveat: when adjusted p-values are exactly zero (which occurs for genes with extremely strong differential expression, where the p-value underflows to zero in floating-point arithmetic), `−log10(0)` is infinite. In practice, this is handled by replacing zero p-values with the smallest non-zero p-value in the distribution (or a machine epsilon floor), which caps the rank score at a finite maximum. This rank ceiling means that the top-ranked genes are not perfectly differentiated by their rank scores — they all receive the same maximum score — but this does not affect the GSEA result, because GSEA uses the rank order rather than the absolute rank values. The ceiling affects only the relative ordering among the very top genes, which are all strongly upregulated and would contribute equally to pathway enrichment regardless of their exact rank.

---

## 9.9 Overall Analytical Decisions — Summary Table

| Decision | Choice made | Alternative considered | Reason for choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dataset | GSE149614 (Lu *et al.*, 2022) | Other HCC datasets | Large, well-annotated, expert labels available for validation |
| Samples | HCC01T, HCC02T, HCC03T (primary tumour only) | All 21 samples | Clinically relevant compartment; computationally tractable; multi-patient for batch assessment |
| Gene of interest | AFP | Other HCC markers (GPC3, EPCAM) | Canonical clinical biomarker; highly cluster-specific; well-established biology |
| Doublet detection | scDblFinder, per-sample | No doublet detection; DoubletFinder | Simulation-based; per-sample run avoids inter-patient false positives |
| MT% threshold | ≤10% | ≤5%, ≤20% | Matches original paper; appropriate for liver/tumour tissue |
| Gene count upper bound | ≤8,000 | ≤6,000 (conservative), ≤5,000 | Hepatocytes are highly transcriptionally complex; 8,000 matches the original paper and avoids removing genuine high-quality malignant hepatocytes; scDblFinder handles doublet removal |
| Normalisation | SCTransform v2 | Log-normalisation | Superior variance stabilisation; removes depth confounding without arbitrary scaling |
| Batch assessment | LISI score on PCA | Visual inspection only | Quantitative; objective threshold; reproducible decision |
| Integration | Harmony (conditional on LISI) | No integration; Seurat CCA | Fast; operates on PCA; effective for patient-level effects |
| PCs used | 30 | 20, 50 | Elbow plot inflection; standard for ~10k cells |
| Clustering resolution | 0.6 (primary) | 0.4, 0.8 | Tested all three; 0.6 balances granularity and interpretability |
| Annotation method | Direct marker-gene assignment from FindAllMarkers output (top avg_log2FC per cluster, padj < 0.05) | Hierarchical mean-expression scoring (failed — all clusters classified as non-immune); SingleR; manual inspection | FindAllMarkers fold-change is computed relative to all other clusters, correcting for ambient RNA and sparsity; unambiguous for all 15 clusters; validated against published labels with >90% agreement |
| Pathway enrichment | GSEA (bidirectional) | ORA | Uses full ranked list; detects both activated and suppressed pathways |
| GSEA ranking metric | sign(log2FC) × −log10(padj) | log2FC alone; −log10(padj) alone | Combines effect size and significance; handles zero p-values robustly |

---

## Session Information

```{r session-info}
sessionInfo()
```

---

*Analysis performed using Seurat v5, clusterProfiler, Harmony, and scDblFinder. Dataset: GSE149614, Lu et al., Nat Commun 13, 4594 (2022). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-32283-3*
